This is the new analytical contribution of this preregistration, and it reports two findings. The headline finding asks how well the field of approaches predicts the experiment at all: the distribution of predictive quality across submissions on each metric, its centre (mean, median), its spread, and how close the field comes to the human replication reference. The secondary finding ranks individual approaches against one another. It is a structural read of which approaches lead on which rung, deliberately not a hunt for a single champion.

Both findings score every submission with the same metric ladder specified under Comparison functions above, in increasing order of strictness: directional agreement, Spearman ρ, Pearson r (with the noise-corrected radj as its companion), inferential agreement, RMSE, and TOST equivalence. This lets approaches be compared directly and against the human replication reference. Knowing on which rung an approach breaks is more informative than any single pass/fail verdict. On top of the ladder, the calibration regression (human ATEs on predicted ATEs) separates additive bias (α) from proportionality (β). Distributional metrics (variance ratio, overlap coefficient, KS distance) test whether Tier 1 submissions reproduce response shapes, not just means. The stereotyping diagnostics ask whether demographic variables predict synthetic responses with the same magnitude as in humans, or whether simulated respondents over-rely on demographic cues.

Scoring principle: one score per metric, pooled across outcomes, in one unit. Each approach is scored on every rung of the ladder, with the intervention × outcome estimates pooled across all 13 outcomes into a single value per metric: the 11 continuous sliders plus the two behavioral outcomes (donation via the OLS model, newsletter signup via logistic marginal effects on the probability scale, as in v1’s pooled row). Before pooling, every estimate and standard error is converted to percentage points (pp) of its outcome’s scale range — the unit of the predicted-vs-observed analysis of Ashokkumar et al. (2026), whose primary archive pools effects only after this conversion and who avoid SD standardization because synthetic under-dispersion corrupts an SD denominator (the same rationale applies here). Pooling the raw mixed units instead would not be neutral: dollar and probability effects are numerically 10–100× smaller than slider effects, so the sliders would dominate every pooled correlation, and the behavioral outcomes would collapse into a tight cluster at the origin that any submission — including one predicting zero for them — gets “right” for free. The conversion is a modelling choice, stated plainly: it equates 3 points on a 0–100 slider with $0.30 on the donation and 3 pp of signup probability, the same equation the TOST bound already makes. We deliberately do not collapse the rungs into one composite “champion” score. An approach can recover the direction of effects well while mis-scaling them, or do well on the primary outcome while failing on the rest, and a single number would hide exactly the structure the benchmark exists to reveal. Results are therefore read per metric: each panel is its own story. Pooling across outcomes (rather than averaging per-outcome scores) keeps every intervention × outcome comparison as one data point and matches the pooled-row logic of the ATE analysis in Section 1. Pooling has one known artifact, and we report its diagnostic: a pooled correlation can be inflated by a submission that predicts which outcomes are generally movable while misranking the interventions within every outcome. The leaderboard therefore carries the Fisher-z average of the per-outcome correlations beside the pooled value (fisher_z_avg()). When the two diverge, that is the signature of exactly this artifact, and the per-outcome breakdown shows where. With all estimates in pp, RMSE and its noise-corrected companion RMSEadj are comparable across outcomes and are reported pooled as well as per outcome (in v1’s raw-unit, per-outcome framing they could not be pooled). The per-intervention breakdown keeps its restriction to outcomes_continuous: the pp conversion removes the unit mix, but a per-intervention correlation across all 13 outcomes would still mix attitude and behavioral constructs, so the conservative subset is retained.

Unit of analysis and coverage rules. Teams may enter up to two submissions per tier, so submissions are not independent draws of “an approach”: sibling entries from one team would overweight prolific teams in the field distribution and inflate its maximum. The field statistics (Finding 1) therefore use one pre-designated primary entry per team. Every valid entry still appears on the leaderboard (Finding 2), labelled primary or secondary. Declared coverage subsets are permitted, but a self-chosen easy subset could top a pooled correlation without being comparable to full-coverage entries. The primary leaderboard is therefore restricted to full-coverage entries. Partial-coverage entries are reported in a separate annex, scored pairwise on what they cover, with their coverage stated. Finally, the field distribution describes an invited, self-selected set of expert teams: a purposive sample of the current generation of serious approaches, not a random sample of all possible ones. We state this wherever the field summary is interpreted, and we do not display a field summary at all if fewer than five primary entries are scored.

Presentation. Every preregistered metric is computed and reported, but a figure that shows six metrics at once shows none of them well, so the figures carry only one key metric per question, stated here once and used consistently throughout. Pearson r is the key metric for ATE recovery (Section 1) and, on the same axis, for subgroup recovery (Section 2). The calibration slope β and intercept α answer whether those effects are the right size, not only correctly ranked. The variance ratio is the key metric for response-shape recovery (Section 1b/2b). Every other rung of the preregistered ladder (directional agreement, Spearman ρ, the adjusted correlations, inferential and TOST agreement, RMSE, OVL, KS, W1) is still computed and scored for every submission, and reported in a companion table beside each figure. Nothing is dropped, only de-emphasized in the plots.

The headline display (Figure 1) puts the key measures in one figure with two panels. Panel A plots predicted against human ATEs across all approaches and outcomes, in percentage points of each outcome’s scale range. This is the direct visual of the prediction task, in the units of the predicted-vs-observed figure of Ashokkumar et al. (2026) (effects on the original scales, no SD standardization, which an under-dispersed submission would corrupt). It is overlaid with the average field and human calibration regressions and a corner readout of their correlation and calibration coefficients (slope β, perfect = 1, and intercept α, perfect = 0, with 95% CIs). Panel B ranks the approaches on the pooled Pearson r, each with a cluster-bootstrap uncertainty interval over interventions (introduced by this preregistration, since v1 reported point estimates only, and detailed under Uncertainty intervals below), with the human replication reference pinned on top and an all-approaches summary row at the bottom carrying the field’s density and mean with its CI. This one panel carries both the field distribution (Finding 1) and the ranking (Finding 2). Three further figures break the ATE and shape key metrics down: by outcome and by intervention (Figure 2), by moderator for subgroup recovery (Figure 3), and per-outcome response densities with the variance ratio and Wasserstein-1 distance stamped on each panel (Figure 4). Every entry carries its disclosure-class badge (open / escrowed / sealed) in the leaderboard table, and the precise numbers accompany every figure as a companion table.

Uncertainty intervals on the leaderboard

Why the leaderboard carries intervals at all. Each point in the ranking panel is a single pooled score, one number per approach per metric. A bare point invites over-reading: if one approach scores Pearson r = 0.60 and another 0.55, is the first genuinely better, or is the gap an artifact of the particular interventions that happened to be tested? The interval answers exactly that question. It is the guard against ranking approaches on differences that would not survive a different, equally reasonable test.

What the interval is, mechanically. The pooled metric is computed once on the full data to give the point estimate. We then form a cluster bootstrap over interventions: the 16 interventions are resampled with replacement (some drawn twice, some dropped), the pooled metric is recomputed on each resampled set, and the 2.5th–97.5th percentiles of those recomputed scores give the 95% interval (cluster_boot() in R/functions/statistics.R). Clustering is by intervention (each intervention’s full block of outcome rows is drawn or dropped together) because the outcome rows belonging to one intervention are not independent. No submitted prediction is ever altered: each approach’s predicted effect for each intervention stays fixed at the value the team submitted. What is resampled is which interventions enter the score, not how well anyone did on them. The interval therefore measures the spread of the recomputed score, not the spread of the raw effects: averaging or correlating cancels much of the effect-to-effect spread, which is why the bar is typically far narrower than the range of the underlying estimates.

What the interval means, and the one assumption it rests on. The interval treats the 16 interventions as a test set standing in for the broader space of interventions the benchmark cares about, exactly as a held-out test set stands in for future cases in predictive modelling. Read this way, the bar answers a generalisation question: would this approach’s score, and its rank, survive on a different set of interventions of the same kind? This is the appropriate question for a benchmark, whose purpose is to identify general predictive skill rather than performance on sixteen specific stimuli. The assumption is therefore about the interventions, not the predictions: that the tested set is a sample from a population of possible interventions. Resampling does not assume that a team that predicted a given intervention well “could have” gotten it wrong (the predictions are fixed). It assumes only that a replication might have tested a different set of interventions. (If one instead regarded these exact sixteen as the entire universe of interest, there would be no sampling and hence no uncertainty to display. The scores would simply be exact. We do not adopt that reading: the benchmark exists to compare approaches, not to characterise these particular stimuli.)

Interval width is informative, not a penalty on accuracy. Because the metrics are bounded agreement scores with a ceiling at perfect agreement, an approach that tracks the human effects closely sits on the diagonal and stays there under resampling: dropping or duplicating interventions barely moves a score already pinned near perfect agreement. Accurate approaches therefore carry narrow intervals. An approach whose predictions do not track the human effects has a score floating in the interior, free to swing with the resampled set, and so carries a wide interval. Interval width is thus entangled with accuracy, but in the helpful direction: a wide bar is the leaderboard signalling that a score is flimsy and may reflect test-set luck rather than skill, precisely the warning one wants attached to a weak entry. (Spread in the size of the true effects does not by itself widen the bar. The correlation and agreement metrics are scale-free, so what matters is whether predictions hug the line, not how far apart the interventions are. Width also partly reflects differences in per-intervention difficulty, so it is a flimsiness signal, not a pure standard error.)

Decision rule for ranking claims: paired differences on shared replicates. Overlapping marginal bootstrap intervals do not mean “A and B are indistinguishable”, and non-overlap is not required to claim a difference. That is the standard misreading. Because every submission is scored against the same reference on the same intervention clusters with the same preregistered bootstrap seed, the correct comparison is paired: draw one set of clusters per replicate, score both submissions on it, and take the difference, so the shared reference noise cancels within each replicate (cluster_boot_diff()). We preregister the rule that any claim that one approach outperforms another on a metric requires the paired-difference 95% interval to exclude zero. Otherwise the two are reported as statistically indistinguishable on that metric, the most common outcome among top performers in comparable mass-collaboration benchmarks (Salganik et al. 2020), and one we expect here. Two caveats apply to all bootstrap intervals: with 16 intervention clusters, percentile intervals are known to undercover somewhat, and the 16 ATEs share the single control arm, which induces positive dependence the intervention-level resampling does not capture. The intervals are read as honest approximations, not exact coverage statements.

Relation to the per-effect confidence and uncertainty intervals. The bootstrap interval is a different layer of uncertainty from the confidence intervals (Tier 1) and uncertainty intervals (Tiers 2–3) attached to individual treatment effects, and the two should not be conflated. A per-effect CI or PI is within-intervention: it says how precisely a single effect is known, whether from finite-respondent sampling noise for a Tier 1 model fit or from the team’s own stated uncertainty for a Tier 2/3 submission. The bootstrap interval is across-intervention: it says how much the pooled score depends on which interventions were tested. The two are nested, not competing. The per-effect intervals enter the value of each row. For the correlation and directional rungs only the point estimates are used, so the per-effect intervals do not affect those scores at all and the bootstrap is their sole source of an error bar. For the inferential-agreement and TOST rungs the per-effect intervals set each row’s significance or equivalence label, which the bootstrap then pools and resamples. In all cases the bootstrap holds each intervention’s estimate fixed at its point value: it propagates the which-interventions uncertainty into the bars but not the within-effect sampling noise.

A noted alternative. Folding both layers into a single bar would require a two-level bootstrap: in each replicate, resample the interventions and perturb each drawn effect by its standard error (drawing the effect from a normal centred on the estimate with that SE). We adopt the single-level (point-estimate) cluster bootstrap as the default because, with only sixteen interventions, the which-interventions term dominates the uncertainty in any ranking, while the human study is powered such that each effect’s standard error is comparatively small relative to the treatment-to-treatment spread. The per-effect CIs and PIs remain reported in the per-outcome breakdowns as the finer, within-effect layer.

Scope across panels. The ATE leaderboard scores interventions, so its bootstrap resamples interventions and is available to every tier: the resampling needs only one predicted effect per intervention, which all tiers supply (including effect-only Tier 3). The subgroup and distributional leaderboards are restricted to Tier 1, but for a different reason: their metrics (interaction models, response-shape comparisons) require respondent-level data, not the bootstrap. The distributional metrics are computed in the control condition only and have no intervention contrast, so that panel necessarily resamples outcomes rather than interventions. With a handful of outcomes those intervals are correspondingly coarser and are read with that caveat.

Registered approach characteristics (open- vs. closed-weight, persona source, administration protocol, fine-tuned vs. not, …) are joined to the leaderboard for a descriptive, hypothesis-generating analysis of which design choices accompany better predictions. This analysis is preregistered as exploratory and restricted to publicly disclosed features (Class C entries are excluded from this analysis, not from the leaderboard).

Note

Exploratory by design. With a modest number of teams, the relationship between approach characteristics and predictive performance is reported descriptively (rankings, group comparisons, visual patterns). We do not preregister inferential tests of feature effects. The cross-team feature analysis is hypothesis-generating, intended to surface which design choices appear to matter for future, larger benchmarks.

Scoring functions

The cross-team analysis reuses the comparison functions specified under Comparison functions, above, and adds the scoring helpers below (in R/functions/statistics.R), each shown in full and explained before it.

pooled_metrics() computes the ATE-recovery ladder on intervention × outcome pairs pooled across all outcomes: directional agreement, Spearman ρ, Pearson r, the noise-corrected companion radj (via adjusted_metrics(), see Comparison functions), the Fisher-z per-outcome-averaged correlations (via fisher_z_avg(), the pooled-vs-averaged divergence diagnostic, see Scoring principle), inferential agreement, and TOST equivalence with the fixed bound (tost_delta, a uniform 3 pp in the cross-team scoring). It mirrors the original compare_estimates() but operates on the pooled set of pairs rather than a single outcome. The pairs it receives are already in pp of scale range (converted once, at pair-building time), so include_rmse = TRUE adds RMSE and RMSEadj in pp — used for the pooled leaderboard as well as the per-outcome breakdown. It expects pre-joined pairs with the reference (Human 1) columns estimate_h, se_h, p_adj_h and the submission columns estimate_l, se_l, infer_l. pi_metrics() scores the Tier-2/3 uncertainty intervals (coverage + Winkler interval score). cluster_boot_diff() implements the paired-replicate decision rule for ranking claims.

Code 
print_a_function_from_file("pooled_metrics")
pooled_metrics <- function(pairs, tost_delta = 3, include_rmse = FALSE) {
  delta_of <- function(outcome) {
    if (length(tost_delta) == 1 && is.null(names(tost_delta))) tost_delta
    else unname(tost_delta[outcome])
  }
  scored <- pairs |>
    mutate(
      delta      = delta_of(outcome),
      diff       = estimate_h - estimate_l,
      se_diff    = sqrt(se_h^2 + se_l^2),
      p_tost     = pmax(pnorm((diff + delta) / se_diff, lower.tail = FALSE),
                        pnorm((diff - delta) / se_diff)),
      equivalent = p_tost < 0.05,
      same_sign  = sign(estimate_h) == sign(estimate_l),
      infer_h    = case_when(
        !is.na(p_adj_h) & p_adj_h < .05 & estimate_h > 0 ~ "pos",
        !is.na(p_adj_h) & p_adj_h < .05 & estimate_h < 0 ~ "neg",
        TRUE                                             ~ "ns"
      )
    )
  adj <- adjusted_metrics(pairs)
  out <- scored |>
    summarise(
      directional_pct = mean(same_sign, na.rm = TRUE) * 100,
      spearman_rho    = cor(estimate_h, estimate_l, method = "spearman",
                            use = "pairwise.complete.obs"),
      pearson_r       = cor(estimate_h, estimate_l, use = "pairwise.complete.obs"),
      inferential_pct = mean(infer_h == infer_l, na.rm = TRUE) * 100,
      tost_pct        = mean(equivalent, na.rm = TRUE) * 100
    ) |>
    mutate(
      pearson_adj = adj$pearson_adj,
      # Fisher-z average of the per-outcome correlations: guards the pooled
      # correlation against between-outcome inflation (a submission that only
      # predicts which OUTCOMES move, while misranking interventions within
      # every outcome, scores high pooled but low here — divergence between
      # the two is the diagnostic).
      pearson_fz  = fisher_z_avg(pairs, method = "pearson"),
      spearman_fz = fisher_z_avg(pairs, method = "spearman")
    ) |>
    relocate(pearson_adj, .after = pearson_r)
  if (include_rmse)
    out <- out |>
      mutate(rmse = sqrt(mean((pairs$estimate_h - pairs$estimate_l)^2, na.rm = TRUE)),
             rmse_adj = adj$rmse_adj)
  out
}

signed_metrics() is the reduced ladder for the subgroup leaderboard, where only the signed-effect rungs are pooled (directional agreement, Spearman ρ, Pearson r). This matches Section 2, above, which reports these three on estimate-only pairs (no standard error or inferential category).

Code 
print_a_function_from_file("signed_metrics")
signed_metrics <- function(pairs) {
  out <- pairs |>
    summarise(
      directional_pct = mean(sign(estimate_h) == sign(estimate_l), na.rm = TRUE) * 100,
      spearman_rho    = cor(estimate_h, estimate_l, method = "spearman",
                            use = "pairwise.complete.obs"),
      pearson_r       = cor(estimate_h, estimate_l, use = "pairwise.complete.obs")
    )
  # Adjusted correlation where the reference SEs travel with the pairs. This is
  # where disattenuation earns its keep: small subgroup cells make reference
  # noise non-uniform, so raw r is unevenly deflated across cells.
  if ("se_h" %in% names(pairs))
    out <- out |> mutate(pearson_adj = adjusted_metrics(pairs)$pearson_adj)
  out
}

cluster_boot() attaches the uncertainty interval described above. It resamples the levels of a clustering variable (interventions for the ATE and subgroup panels, outcomes for the distributional panel) with replacement, recomputes the supplied metric function on each replicate, and returns the point estimate together with percentile confidence bounds in long form (one row per metric, with value, lo, hi). It is generic: the metric function is passed in, so the same bootstrap serves every panel.

Code 
print_a_function_from_file("cluster_boot")
cluster_boot <- function(df, f, cluster = "condition", n_boot = 1000,
                         conf = 0.95, seed = 2026) {
  if (!is.null(seed)) set.seed(seed)

  point <- f(df) |>
    pivot_longer(everything(), names_to = "metric", values_to = "value")

  parts    <- split(df, as.character(df[[cluster]]))
  cl_names <- names(parts)

  boot <- map_dfr(seq_len(n_boot), function(b) {
    drawn <- sample(cl_names, length(cl_names), replace = TRUE)
    f(bind_rows(parts[drawn])) |>
      pivot_longer(everything(), names_to = "metric", values_to = "value")
  })

  alpha <- (1 - conf) / 2
  point |>
    left_join(
      boot |>
        group_by(metric) |>
        summarise(lo = quantile(value, alpha,     na.rm = TRUE),
                  hi = quantile(value, 1 - alpha, na.rm = TRUE),
                  .groups = "drop"),
      by = "metric"
    )
}

metrics_by_group() scores within each level of a grouping variable instead of pooling everything into one number: with group = "outcome" it returns one ladder per outcome (scored across the interventions), and with group = "condition" one ladder per intervention (scored across the outcomes). It drives the by-outcome and by-intervention distribution figures. Per intervention the score runs across outcomes_continuous only: with all pairs in pp the scales no longer differ, but a correlation across all 13 outcomes within one intervention would still mix attitude and behavioral constructs, so the conservative subset is kept.

Code 
print_a_function_from_file("metrics_by_group")
metrics_by_group <- function(pairs, group, f = pooled_metrics) {
  pairs |>
    group_by(.data[[group]]) |>
    # .keep = TRUE: f may need the grouping column itself (e.g. the per-outcome
    # TOST bound lookup in pooled_metrics)
    group_modify(~ f(.x), .keep = TRUE) |>
    ungroup()
}

summarise_field() reduces the per-approach scores to the field-level summary printed beside the headline figure: mean, median, sd, min, and max of each metric across approaches (excluding the reference row).

Code 
print_a_function_from_file("summarise_field")
summarise_field <- function(field_long, ceiling_label = "Human replication") {
  field_long |>
    filter(submission != ceiling_label) |>
    group_by(metric) |>
    summarise(
      mean   = mean(value,   na.rm = TRUE),
      median = median(value, na.rm = TRUE),
      sd     = sd(value,     na.rm = TRUE),
      min    = min(value,    na.rm = TRUE),
      max    = max(value,    na.rm = TRUE),
      .groups = "drop"
    )
}

The remainder of this section demonstrates the code on placeholder data: 30 mock “teams”, each a stratified bootstrap resample (within condition) of the single individual-level placeholder dataset, stacked with a team label, enough approaches to give the field distributions a meaningful shape. To exercise all three tier code paths, 28 teams enter at Tier 1 (individual-level), one is collapsed into a Tier 2 cell-level file, and one into a Tier 3 effect-level file. All of this placeholder data, including the resampled teams and the collapsed Tier 2/3 files, is built in the simulation script (R/simulate_data_preregistration.R), not here. This document only reads it. Because every mock team is just a resample of the same dataset, all approaches are random noise relative to the human reference. On placeholder data the field distributions therefore cluster near the null, and the figures only show that the code runs. Real submissions will spread out. The adjusted correlation radj behaves accordingly: with no true signal, the reference effects’ observed spread is all noise, so its guard (estimated true variance ≤ 0 → NA) binds for many mock teams and the surviving values are unstable. That is the intended behaviour, demonstrated rather than hidden. The code applies to real submissions without modification.

Code 
outcomes_primary   <- "trust_multidimensional"
outcomes_secondary <- c("trust_post", "distrust_post",
                         "funding_perceptions", "policy_role_mean", "inst_trust_mean")
outcomes_tertiary  <- c("belief_post", "concern_mean", "policy_general",
                        "policy_specific_mean", "behavior_mean")
outcomes_continuous <- c(outcomes_primary, outcomes_secondary, outcomes_tertiary)

# The two behavioral outcomes enter the pooled cross-team ladder alongside the
# 0-100 sliders (as in v1's pooled ATE row): donation in dollars via the OLS
# model, newsletter signup via logistic marginal effects on the probability
# scale. Scale-bound quantities (RMSE, the Winkler interval score, the
# by-intervention ladder) stay on `outcomes_continuous` only.
outcomes_behavioral <- c("donation_ams", "newsletter_signup")
outcomes_pooled     <- c(outcomes_continuous, outcomes_behavioral)

# Scale ranges: all cross-team scoring runs in percentage points (pp) of each
# outcome's scale range — effects and SEs are converted once, at pair-building
# time, and every downstream metric, figure, and table reads the same unit.
# This follows Ashokkumar et al.'s primary archive, which pools effects only
# after expressing them in pp of the original scales, and deliberately avoids
# SD standardization (an under-dispersed submission would corrupt an SD
# denominator). Pooling raw mixed units instead would let the 0-100 sliders
# drown out the two behavioral outcomes (dollar and probability effects are
# 10-100x smaller numerically) and hand every submission 32 free near-origin
# points. The 0-100 sliders are already in pp; only the donation (0-10
# dollars) and signup probability (0-1) rescale.
scale_range <- c(
  set_names(rep(100, length(outcomes_continuous)), outcomes_continuous),
  donation_ams = 10, newsletter_signup = 1
)

# Fixed, prespecified TOST equivalence bound: 3% of each outcome's scale
# range, which in pp units is a uniform 3 (3 points on the 0-100 sliders,
# $0.30 on the 0-10 donation, 3 percentage points on the signup proportion).
# Fixed rather than |ATE_h|-relative (v1's bound): a bound computed from the
# noisy estimate under test is not a valid alpha-level procedure, and the
# design analysis below shows the relative bound has no dynamic range at this
# design's reference SEs.
tost_delta_pp <- 3

# Bootstrap replicates: the PREREGISTERED value for the real-data run is
# 2,000. This placeholder render is a demonstration of the procedure, not of
# its precision, so it uses a small demo value to keep the render cheap —
# only this parameter changes when the submissions arrive, nothing else.
n_boot_real <- 2000
n_boot_demo <- 200

# Illustrative outcome subset and categorical moderators — same definitions as
# Section 2 above. The subgroup-effects leaderboard scores the illustrative
# outcomes plus the binary newsletter signup (see Section 2: its interaction
# difference-in-differences come from the linear probability model, so they
# live on the probability scale and pp-rescale like everything else).
outcomes_illustrative <- c("trust_multidimensional", "donation_ams",
                           "funding_perceptions", "policy_general")
outcomes_subgroup     <- c(outcomes_illustrative, "newsletter_signup")
moderators_cat <- c("gender", "age_band", "race", "education", "income", "party")

outcome_label_map <- c(
  trust_multidimensional = "Multidimensional trust (primary)",
  trust_post             = "Overall trust",
  distrust_post          = "Distrust",
  funding_perceptions    = "Funding perceptions",
  policy_role_mean       = "Policy role",
  inst_trust_mean        = "Institutional trust",
  belief_post            = "Climate belief",
  concern_mean           = "Climate concern",
  policy_general         = "General climate policy",
  policy_specific_mean   = "Specific climate policy",
  behavior_mean          = "Pro-climate behavior",
  donation_ams           = "Donation to AMS ($)",
  newsletter_signup      = "Newsletter signup"
)
Code 
# Extract ATE pairs (estimate, SE, adjusted p) — same helper as the original
# preregistration's rq1-all chunk.
prep_ate <- function(df) {
  df |>
    select(condition, estimate) |>
    mutate(
      se    = if ("std.error"        %in% names(df)) df$std.error        else NA_real_,
      p_adj = if ("p.value_adjusted" %in% names(df)) df$p.value_adjusted else NA_real_
    )
}

# Inferential category from estimate sign + adjusted p-value (Tiers 1-2)
classify_sig <- function(estimate, p_adj) {
  case_when(
    !is.na(p_adj) & p_adj < .05 & estimate > 0 ~ "pos",
    !is.na(p_adj) & p_adj < .05 & estimate < 0 ~ "neg",
    TRUE                                       ~ "ns"
  )
}

control_lab <- levels(human_data$condition)[1]
z95 <- qnorm(0.975)

# Reference (Human 1) ATE models on the continuous outcomes, fit once and
# reused (the calibration regression reads the primary outcome's model).
human_models <- map(outcomes_continuous,
                    ~ run_main_treatment_model(human_data_1, outcome = .x)) |>
  set_names(outcomes_continuous)

# One ATE side (estimate, SE, BH-adjusted p) for an individual-level dataset,
# across all pooled outcomes: OLS with HC2 SEs for the continuous outcomes and
# the donation; logistic marginal effects on the probability scale for the
# binary newsletter signup (as in v1's pooled row). The binary SE is recovered
# from the marginal-effect CI.
ate_side <- function(dat, models = NULL) {
  cont <- map_dfr(c(outcomes_continuous, "donation_ams"), function(out) {
    res <- if (!is.null(models) && out %in% names(models)) models[[out]]
           else run_main_treatment_model(dat, outcome = out)
    prep_ate(res) |> mutate(outcome = out)
  })
  bin <- run_main_treatment_model_binary(dat,
                                         outcome = "newsletter_signup")$marginal_effects |>
    transmute(condition,
              estimate,
              se      = (conf.high - conf.low) / (2 * z95),
              p_adj   = p.value_adjusted,
              outcome = "newsletter_signup")
  bind_rows(cont, bin)
}

human_side <- ate_side(human_data_1, models = human_models) |>
  rename(estimate_h = estimate, se_h = se, p_adj_h = p_adj)

# Common unit for all scoring: estimates and SEs in pp of each outcome's scale
# range (see `scale_range` in the variables chunk). Applied once to every set
# of joined pairs — the three tier builders, the floor baseline, and the
# subgroup pairs — so all pooled metrics, figures, and tables read one unit.
# A positive multiplicative factor: signs, p-values, and inferential
# categories are untouched.
pp_scale_pairs <- function(pairs) {
  pairs |>
    mutate(across(any_of(c("estimate_h", "se_h", "estimate_l", "se_l")),
                  ~ .x * 100 / scale_range[outcome]))
}

# Human per-condition cell sizes (named vector), for standardizing Tier-1
# submission-side inference to the human design's precision.
n_cells_h <- human_data_1 |> count(condition) |> deframe()

# Tier 1: pool ATE pairs from an individual-level dataset by refitting the
# preregistered treatment models on the submission. Submission-side INFERENCE
# (the inferential-agreement category and the TOST SE) is evaluated at the
# HUMAN design's per-arm precision: a team may simulate any pool size, so
# model-based significance would otherwise measure that choice rather than the
# method — and the Tier-1 precision requirement would mechanically hurt
# precise teams wherever a human effect is n.s. Each submission SE is rescaled
# by the two-cell design factor sqrt((1/n_h,k + 1/n_h,ctrl) /
# (1/n_l,k + 1/n_l,ctrl)) and p-values recomputed (normal approximation, BH
# within outcome, mirroring the Tier-2 path). Point estimates are untouched,
# so the correlation, directional, and RMSE rungs are unaffected. This also
# makes the inferential and TOST columns comparable across tiers: every
# submission's "significance" is evaluated at the same design precision.
build_pairs_t1 <- function(pred_data) {
  n_cells_l <- pred_data |> count(condition) |> deframe()
  fac <- sqrt((1 / n_cells_h + 1 / n_cells_h[[control_lab]]) /
              (1 / n_cells_l[names(n_cells_h)] + 1 / n_cells_l[[control_lab]]))
  ate_side(pred_data) |>
    mutate(se_std = se * unname(fac[condition])) |>
    group_by(outcome) |>
    mutate(p_adj = p.adjust(2 * pnorm(-abs(estimate / se_std)), method = "BH")) |>
    ungroup() |>
    mutate(infer_l = classify_sig(estimate, p_adj)) |>
    select(condition, outcome, estimate_l = estimate, se_l = se_std, infer_l) |>
    inner_join(human_side, by = c("condition", "outcome")) |>
    pp_scale_pairs()
}

# Tier 2: ATEs and SEs from submitted cell means and their 95% prediction
# intervals (mean, pi_lower, pi_upper per condition x outcome). Each cell
# interval implies an SE on the cell mean; the treatment and control cells
# combine for the between-subjects contrast. Normal-approximation p-values,
# BH-adjusted within outcome to mirror the Tier 1 models.
build_pairs_t2 <- function(cells_main) {
  cells_main |>
    mutate(
      condition = as.character(condition),
      se_cell   = (pi_upper - pi_lower) / (2 * z95)
    ) |>
    group_by(outcome) |>
    group_modify(function(cells, key) {
      ctrl <- cells |> filter(condition == control_lab)
      cells |>
        filter(condition != control_lab) |>
        mutate(
          estimate_l = mean - ctrl$mean,
          se_l       = sqrt(se_cell^2 + ctrl$se_cell^2),
          p_adj      = p.adjust(2 * pnorm(-abs(estimate_l / se_l)), method = "BH"),
          infer_l    = classify_sig(estimate_l, p_adj)
        ) |>
        select(condition, estimate_l, se_l, infer_l)
    }) |>
    ungroup() |>
    inner_join(human_side, by = c("condition", "outcome")) |>
    pp_scale_pairs()
}

# Tier 3: submitted ATEs taken as-is; inference from the submitted 95%
# uncertainty interval (significant iff the interval excludes zero, the direct
# analog of a two-sided test at alpha = .05); implied SE from the interval
# width for the TOST computation.
build_pairs_t3 <- function(effects) {
  effects |>
    mutate(
      condition  = as.character(condition),
      estimate_l = ate,
      se_l       = (pi_upper - pi_lower) / (2 * z95),
      infer_l    = case_when(
        pi_lower > 0 ~ "pos",
        pi_upper < 0 ~ "neg",
        TRUE         ~ "ns"
      )
    ) |>
    inner_join(human_side, by = c("condition", "outcome")) |>
    select(condition, outcome, estimate_l, se_l, infer_l,
           estimate_h, se_h, p_adj_h) |>
    pp_scale_pairs()
}

# `pooled_metrics()`, `signed_metrics()`, and `cluster_boot()` are defined in
# R/functions/statistics.R and shown under "Scoring functions" above.

# One leaderboard row: pooled ladder + calibration slope and R^2 on the
# primary outcome (HC2-robust inference: the outcome side of the calibration
# regression carries known, condition-varying sampling error) + the two
# interval scores for Tiers 2-3 (pi_metrics(); the Winkler score is
# scale-bound, so it runs on the 0-100 outcomes only). Interval scores are NA
# for Tier 1 (its SEs are sampling, not epistemic, uncertainty) and for the
# reference rows.
leaderboard_row <- function(pairs, submission, tier, disclosure) {
  calib <- run_calibration(
    human_models[[outcomes_primary]],
    pairs |> filter(outcome == outcomes_primary) |>
      select(condition, estimate = estimate_l),
    robust = TRUE
  )
  pi <- if (tier %in% c("2", "3"))
    pi_metrics(pairs |> filter(outcome %in% outcomes_continuous))
  else tibble(pi_coverage_pct = NA_real_, interval_score = NA_real_)
  pooled_metrics(pairs, tost_delta = tost_delta_pp, include_rmse = TRUE) |>
    mutate(submission = submission, tier = tier, disclosure = disclosure,
           beta = calib$beta, calib_r2 = calib$r_squared,
           pi_coverage_pct = pi$pi_coverage_pct,
           interval_score  = pi$interval_score)
}
Code 
# Mock Tier 2 (cell-level) and Tier 3 (effect-level) submissions, generated
# outside this script alongside the other placeholder data (see
# R/simulate_data_preregistration.R) so that no mock-data simulation lives in
# the preregistration itself. Tier 2 collapses one placeholder team to the
# cell-level schema (*_cells_main.csv); Tier 3 gives ATEs vs. control with a
# widened 95% uncertainty interval standing in for a team's epistemic uncertainty.
cells_t2   <- readRDS(here("data/simulation_preregistration/mock_submission_tier2_cells.rds"))
effects_t3 <- readRDS(here("data/simulation_preregistration/mock_submission_tier3_effects.rds"))
Code 
# 30 mock submissions plus the fixed human replication reference (Human 1 vs. Human 2).
# Teams 1-28 enter as individual-level Tier 1 data; team 29 as a Tier 2
# cell-level file and team 30 as a Tier 3 effect-level file, so all three tier
# code paths are exercised. Each submission's ATE pairs are built once here and
# reused by the headline and breakdown figures, the leaderboard table, and the
# companion tables.
tier1_ids <- 1:28

# Cached: the 29 Tier-1 builds each refit the treatment models incl. the
# logistic marginal effects for the newsletter outcome, the slow step of a
# cold render.
pairs_list <- cached("amendment_pairs_list", {
  c(
    set_names(
      map(tier1_ids,
          ~ build_pairs_t1(llm_data_teams |> filter(team == paste0("team_", .x)))),
      paste0("Team ", tier1_ids)
    ),
    list(
      "Team 29"           = build_pairs_t2(cells_t2),
      "Team 30"           = build_pairs_t3(effects_t3),
      "Human replication" = build_pairs_t1(human_data_2)
    )
  )
})

set.seed(2026)
submission_meta <- tibble(
  submission = c(paste0("Team ", 1:30), "Human replication"),
  tier       = c(rep("1", 28), "2", "3", "—"),
  disclosure = c(sample(c("A", "B", "C"), 30, replace = TRUE), "—")
)

# Floor baseline: a naive reference row for the rungs without a natural null
# (inferential %, TOST %, RMSE). "No effect" predicts a zero ATE everywhere —
# a fully naive, blinded-achievable strategy whose inferential agreement is
# whatever the share of truly null human effects hands it for free. It is a
# reference row like the reference, not a member of the field: excluded from the
# field violins and the field summary. (An oracle-mean floor — predict each
# outcome's mean human ATE everywhere — was considered and rejected: the mean
# is the in-sample least-squares constant fit to the very ATEs being scored,
# so it competes rather than floors; its interpretive content survives as the
# RMSE scale reference, the SD of the human ATEs.)
floor_pairs <- list(
  "Floor: no effect" = human_side |>
    mutate(estimate_l = 0, se_l = 0, infer_l = "ns") |>
    pp_scale_pairs()
)
floor_labels <- names(floor_pairs)

# Calibration is undefined for the floor (a constant prediction within the
# primary outcome -> zero-variance regressor), so their rows carry NA there.
# Its directional % reads 0, not 50: sign(0) matches neither sign, an artifact
# of the all-zero prediction, not below-chance skill (footnoted in the table).
floor_rows <- imap_dfr(floor_pairs, function(pr, sub) {
  pooled_metrics(pr, tost_delta = tost_delta_pp, include_rmse = TRUE) |>
    mutate(submission = sub, tier = "—", disclosure = "—",
           beta = NA_real_, calib_r2 = NA_real_,
           pi_coverage_pct = NA_real_, interval_score = NA_real_)
})

leaderboard <- bind_rows(
  imap_dfr(pairs_list, function(pr, sub) {
    m <- submission_meta |> filter(submission == sub)
    leaderboard_row(pr, sub, tier = m$tier, disclosure = m$disclosure)
  }),
  floor_rows
) |>
  arrange(desc(submission == "Human replication"), desc(pearson_r))
Code 
# Display labels / panel order for the metric ladder, and the null reference
# line per metric (50 = chance for directional agreement, 0 for the
# correlations; omitted for the percentage-agreement metrics where no clean
# null exists).
ladder_labels <- c(
  directional_pct = "Directional %",
  spearman_rho    = "Spearman ρ",
  pearson_r       = "Pearson r",
  pearson_adj     = "Pearson r (adj.)",
  inferential_pct = "Inferential %",
  rmse            = "RMSE (pp)",
  rmse_adj        = "RMSE (adj., pp)",
  tost_pct        = "TOST %"
)
ladder_nulls <- c("Directional %" = 50, "Spearman ρ" = 0, "Pearson r" = 0,
                  "Pearson r (adj.)" = 0)

relabel_metrics <- function(df, labels) {
  df |> mutate(metric = factor(labels[metric], levels = labels))
}

Finding 1 — How well can the field predict the experiment?

The headline question is not who wins but how well the current generation of approaches can predict a novel experiment at all. To keep that readable, each question gets one key metric in the figures: Pearson r for ATE recovery, Pearson r on the interaction estimates for subgroup recovery, and the variance ratio for response shape. The correlation is the literature’s common currency (Ashokkumar et al. 2026), and one shared axis across the ATE and subgroup sections makes them directly comparable. The variance ratio carries the field’s one directional expectation (under-dispersion). The full preregistered ladders are computed for everything and reported in the companion tables.

Figure 1 shows the headline: the raw prediction task with the field’s calibration lines and coefficients (A), and the field and its ranking on the pooled Pearson r (B). One rendering note on panel B: this placeholder demonstration runs the bootstrap with a small replicate count (n_boot_demo = 200) to keep the document cheap to build. The preregistered value for the real-data run is n_boot_real = 2,000. When the submissions arrive, only that parameter changes, nothing else about the procedure.

Code 
# (A) the raw prediction task: all approaches, all outcomes. The pairs are
# already in pp of scale range (pp_scale_pairs() at pair-building), the same
# unit as every pooled metric.
headline_pairs <- imap_dfr(pairs_list, ~ mutate(.x, submission = .y))
p_scatter <- plot_pred_scatter(
  headline_pairs,
  x_lab       = "Predicted ATE (pp of scale range)",
  y_lab       = "Human ATE (pp of scale range)",
  tag         = "A",
  point_alpha = 0.15, point_size = 0.5,
  ref_alpha   = 0.6,  ref_size   = 1,
  symmetric   = TRUE
)

# Average field / human calibration lines + a corner readout of correlation
# (r, adj.) and coefficients (β, α with CI). Mean field Pearson r and its
# noise-corrected value come from the pooled leaderboard — the identical pp
# quantity ranked in panel B; the coefficients are fit from the same pp pairs
# by calibration_overlay().
field_ld <- leaderboard |> filter(!submission %in% floor_labels,
                                  submission != "Human replication")
human_ld <- leaderboard |> filter(submission == "Human replication")
p_scatter <- p_scatter +
  calibration_overlay(headline_pairs,
                      r_field    = mean(field_ld$pearson_r,   na.rm = TRUE),
                      radj_field = mean(field_ld$pearson_adj, na.rm = TRUE),
                      r_human    = human_ld$pearson_r,
                      radj_human = human_ld$pearson_adj)

# (B) ranked field on the key metric, with cluster-bootstrap intervals.
# n_boot_demo here; the real-data run uses n_boot_real = 2,000 (see variables).
pooled_f <- \(p) pooled_metrics(p, tost_delta = tost_delta_pp)
ate_forest <- cached("amendment_ate_forest", {
  imap_dfr(pairs_list, function(pr, sub) {
    cluster_boot(pr, pooled_f, cluster = "condition", n_boot = n_boot_demo) |>
      mutate(submission = sub)
  }) |>
    relabel_metrics(ladder_labels)
})
p_forest <- plot_field_forest_single(ate_forest, tag = "B")

(p_scatter | p_forest) + plot_layout(widths = c(1, 1.15))

The full pooled ladder stays scored for every approach. The field’s centre and spread on each rung:

Code 
field_pooled <- leaderboard |>
  filter(!submission %in% floor_labels) |>   # floors are reference rows, not field members
  select(submission, all_of(names(ladder_labels))) |>
  pivot_longer(-submission, names_to = "metric", values_to = "value") |>
  relabel_metrics(ladder_labels)
Code 
summarise_field(field_pooled) |>
  gt() |>
  fmt_number(columns = c(mean, median, sd, min, max), decimals = 2) |>
  cols_label(metric = "Metric", mean = "Mean", median = "Median",
             sd = "SD", min = "Min", max = "Max")
Selecting the best interventions

The correlation rungs summarize the whole effect pattern. A decision-maker’s question is narrower: if I trust this approach and act on its top picks, do I get the interventions that truly work best? topk_selection() scores exactly that (see Section 1): per outcome, each approach’s top k = 3 interventions by predicted ATE (≈ the top 20% of 16 arms, in the spirit of the intervention-selection use case that motivates Ashokkumar et al. (2026)) are compared with the truly best three by the human reference, via the hit rate (share of picks that are in the true best set, chance = 3/16 ≈ 0.19) and the regret ratio (mean true ATE of the picked set as a share of the best achievable, where 1 = perfect pick). The table shows both on the primary outcome and averaged across the continuous outcomes.

Code 
topk_table <- imap_dfr(pairs_list, function(pr, sub) {
  scores <- topk_selection(pr, k = 3)
  bind_cols(
    scores |>
      filter(outcome == outcomes_primary) |>
      transmute(hit_primary = hit_rate, regret_primary = regret_ratio),
    scores |>
      summarise(hit_mean    = mean(hit_rate, na.rm = TRUE),
                regret_mean = mean(regret_ratio, na.rm = TRUE))
  ) |>
    mutate(submission = sub, .before = 1)
}) |>
  arrange(desc(submission == "Human replication"), desc(hit_mean))

topk_table |>
  gt() |>
  fmt_number(columns = c(hit_primary, regret_primary, hit_mean, regret_mean),
             decimals = 2) |>
  sub_missing(missing_text = "—") |>
  cols_label(
    submission     = "Submission",
    hit_primary    = "Hit rate (primary)",
    regret_primary = "Regret ratio (primary)",
    hit_mean       = "Hit rate (mean)",
    regret_mean    = "Regret ratio (mean)"
  ) |>
  tab_style(
    style     = cell_fill(color = "#eef3f7"),
    locations = cells_body(rows = submission == "Human replication")
  )
Where the field predicts well

Split by outcome (one distribution per outcome, scored across the interventions) and by intervention (one per intervention, scored across the 0–100 continuous outcomes — the pp conversion removes the unit mix, but a within-intervention correlation across all 13 outcomes would still mix attitude and behavioral constructs, so the conservative subset is kept), the key metric shows where the field predicts well and what resists prediction (Figure 2). The full ladders per outcome and per intervention are in Table 5 and Table 6.

Code 
# include_rmse: with all pairs in pp, RMSE and its noise-corrected companion
# RMSE_adj are defined per outcome and pooled alike; here they localise where
# the absolute error concentrates.
field_by_outcome <- imap_dfr(pairs_list, function(pr, sub) {
  metrics_by_group(pr, "outcome",
                   f = \(p) pooled_metrics(p, tost_delta = tost_delta_pp,
                                           include_rmse = TRUE)) |>
    mutate(submission = sub)
}) |>
  mutate(outcome = factor(unname(outcome_label_map[outcome]),
                          levels = unname(outcome_label_map))) |>
  pivot_longer(c(directional_pct, spearman_rho, pearson_r, pearson_adj,
                 inferential_pct, rmse, rmse_adj, tost_pct),
               names_to = "metric", values_to = "value") |>
  relabel_metrics(ladder_labels)

# Restricted to the 0-100 continuous outcomes: with pp units the scale
# confound is gone, but a per-intervention correlation across all 13 outcomes
# would still mix constructs (attitude sliders vs. behaviors), so the
# conservative restriction is kept.
field_by_intervention <- imap_dfr(pairs_list, function(pr, sub) {
  metrics_by_group(pr |> filter(outcome %in% outcomes_continuous), "condition") |>
    mutate(submission = sub)
}) |>
  pivot_longer(c(directional_pct, spearman_rho, pearson_r, pearson_adj,
                 inferential_pct, tost_pct),
               names_to = "metric", values_to = "value") |>
  relabel_metrics(ladder_labels)

# Companion-table helper: field median per metric within each group level,
# with the human replication reference value in parentheses.
field_group_table <- function(field_long, group,
                              pct_metrics = c("Directional %", "Inferential %",
                                              "TOST %")) {
  fmt <- function(v, pct) if_else(is.na(v), "—",
                                  if_else(pct, sprintf("%.0f", v),
                                          sprintf("%.2f", v)))
  med <- field_long |>
    filter(submission != "Human replication") |>
    group_by(across(all_of(group)), metric) |>
    summarise(med = median(value, na.rm = TRUE), .groups = "drop")
  ref <- field_long |>
    filter(submission == "Human replication") |>
    select(all_of(group), metric, ref = value)
  med |>
    left_join(ref, by = c(group, "metric")) |>
    mutate(pct  = metric %in% pct_metrics,
           cell = paste0(fmt(med, pct), " (", fmt(ref, pct), ")")) |>
    select(all_of(group), metric, cell) |>
    pivot_wider(names_from = metric, values_from = cell)
}
Code 
by_outcome_r <- field_by_outcome |>
  filter(metric == "Pearson r") |>
  mutate(metric = factor("Outcomes"))
by_intervention_r <- field_by_intervention |>
  filter(metric == "Pearson r") |>
  mutate(metric = factor("Interventions"))

p_by_outcome <- plot_metric_distribution(
  by_outcome_r, x = "outcome", mean_ci = TRUE,
  ref_lines = c("Outcomes" = 0)
) + labs(tag = "A", y = "Pearson r")
p_by_intervention <- plot_metric_distribution(
  by_intervention_r, x = "condition", mean_ci = TRUE,
  ref_lines = c("Interventions" = 0)
) + labs(tag = "B", y = "Pearson r")

p_by_outcome | p_by_intervention
Code 
field_group_table(field_by_outcome, "outcome") |>
  gt() |>
  sub_missing(missing_text = "—") |>
  cols_label(outcome = "Outcome")
Code 
field_group_table(field_by_intervention, "condition") |>
  gt() |>
  sub_missing(missing_text = "—") |>
  cols_label(condition = "Intervention")
Subgroup recovery

Whether an approach reproduces who an intervention moves, not just its average effect. For each Tier-1 submission we fit the preregistered condition × moderator interaction model (run_moderator_model()) across the illustrative outcomes plus the binary newsletter signup, over the six demographic moderators, then pool the interaction estimates against Human 1 (compute_predicted = FALSE skips the marginal-effects step, which the leaderboard does not use). For the binary outcome the same OLS fit is a linear probability model, whose saturated interaction coefficient equals the difference-in-differences of cell signup proportions — the interaction on the probability scale (see the code note below). All interaction estimates are placed in percentage points of each outcome’s scale range before pooling, the same unit as Section 1. Because interaction estimates require respondent-level data, this view covers Tier 1 only. As specified in Section 2 above, the signed-effect rungs are scored (directional agreement, Spearman ρ, Pearson r) plus the adjusted correlation radj, emphasized here because small subgroup cells make the reference noise non-uniform across cells (see Adjusted correlation and RMSE).

Code 
# Human-1 interaction estimates, computed once and reused for every submission.
# The cached() keys keep their historical "amendment_" prefix (the analysis is
# unchanged); they are internal identifiers for analysis/results/*.rds, not tied
# to this file's name, so renaming the document does not require a recompute.
# The binary newsletter outcome runs through the same OLS machinery as a
# linear probability model: in the saturated condition × moderator
# specification the interaction coefficient is exactly the
# difference-in-differences of cell signup proportions — the interaction on
# the probability scale — and the HC2 robust SEs are valid for a binary
# response. It then pp-rescales (× 100) like every other outcome.
human_mod_side <- cached("amendment_human_mod_side", {
  map_dfr(outcomes_subgroup, function(out) {
    map_dfr(moderators_cat, function(mod) {
      run_moderator_model(human_data_1, outcome = out, moderator = mod,
                          compute_predicted = FALSE)$interaction_effects |>
        # se_h feeds the adjusted correlation; only the human reference side
        # is corrected (see "Adjusted correlation and RMSE").
        select(condition, moderator_level,
               estimate_h = estimate, se_h = std.error) |>
        mutate(outcome = out, moderator = mod)
    })
  })
})

build_subgroup_pairs <- function(pred_data) {
  pred_side <- map_dfr(outcomes_subgroup, function(out) {
    map_dfr(moderators_cat, function(mod) {
      run_moderator_model(pred_data, outcome = out, moderator = mod,
                          compute_predicted = FALSE)$interaction_effects |>
        select(condition, moderator_level, estimate_l = estimate) |>
        mutate(outcome = out, moderator = mod)
    })
  })
  inner_join(human_mod_side, pred_side,
             by = c("condition", "moderator_level", "outcome", "moderator")) |>
    pp_scale_pairs()
}

# Tier-1 submissions plus the reference are eligible. Interaction pairs are built
# once per submission and reused by the subgroup figure and its companion table.
subgroup_inputs <- c(
  set_names(
    map(tier1_ids, ~ llm_data_teams |> filter(team == paste0("team_", .x))),
    paste0("Team ", tier1_ids)
  ),
  list("Human replication" = human_data_2)
)
subgroup_pairs_list <- cached("amendment_subgroup_pairs", {
  map(subgroup_inputs, build_subgroup_pairs)
})

Figure 3 shows the moderation analogue of the headline figure: the raw prediction task on the interaction estimates (A), and the key metric per moderator (B), showing which kinds of heterogeneity the field can predict at all. The full signed-effect ladder, pooled, is in Table 7.

Code 
moderator_label_map <- c(gender = "Gender", age_band = "Age",
                         race = "Race/ethnicity", education = "Education",
                         income = "Income", party = "Party ID")

# Pairs are already in pp of scale range (pp_scale_pairs() in the builder).
subgroup_scatter_pairs <- imap_dfr(subgroup_pairs_list,
                                   ~ mutate(.x, submission = .y))
# ~37,000 pooled pairs: much lighter marks than the ATE scatter, or it reads
# as a solid blob.
p_sub_scatter <- plot_pred_scatter(
  subgroup_scatter_pairs,
  x_lab       = "Predicted interaction estimate (pp)",
  y_lab       = "Human interaction estimate (pp)",
  tag         = "A",
  point_alpha = 0.05, point_size = 0.3,
  ref_alpha   = 0.2,  ref_size   = 0.5,
  symmetric   = TRUE
)

# Mean field / human Pearson r on the interaction estimates (from the pooled
# signed-effect ladder, matching panel B), and the calibration lines + readout.
subgroup_ld <- imap_dfr(subgroup_pairs_list,
                        ~ signed_metrics(.x) |> mutate(submission = .y))
sub_field_ld <- subgroup_ld |> filter(submission != "Human replication")
sub_human_ld <- subgroup_ld |> filter(submission == "Human replication")
p_sub_scatter <- p_sub_scatter +
  calibration_overlay(subgroup_scatter_pairs,
                      r_field    = mean(sub_field_ld$pearson_r,   na.rm = TRUE),
                      radj_field = mean(sub_field_ld$pearson_adj, na.rm = TRUE),
                      r_human    = sub_human_ld$pearson_r,
                      radj_human = sub_human_ld$pearson_adj)

field_subgroup_by_mod <- imap_dfr(subgroup_pairs_list, function(pr, sub) {
  pr |>
    group_by(moderator) |>
    group_modify(~ signed_metrics(.x)) |>
    ungroup() |>
    mutate(submission = sub)
}) |>
  transmute(submission,
            value      = pearson_r,
            moderator  = factor(unname(moderator_label_map[moderator]),
                                levels = unname(moderator_label_map)),
            metric     = factor("Moderators"))
p_sub_field <- plot_metric_distribution(
  field_subgroup_by_mod, x = "moderator", mean_ci = TRUE,
  ref_lines = c("Moderators" = 0)
) + labs(tag = "B", y = "Pearson r")

(p_sub_scatter | p_sub_field) + plot_layout(widths = c(1, 1.3))
Code 
field_subgroup <- imap_dfr(subgroup_pairs_list, function(pr, sub) {
  signed_metrics(pr) |>
    pivot_longer(everything(), names_to = "metric", values_to = "value") |>
    mutate(submission = sub)
}) |>
  relabel_metrics(ladder_labels[c("directional_pct", "spearman_rho",
                                  "pearson_r", "pearson_adj")])

summarise_field(field_subgroup) |>
  gt() |>
  fmt_number(columns = c(mean, median, sd, min, max), decimals = 2) |>
  cols_label(metric = "Metric", mean = "Mean", median = "Median",
             sd = "SD", min = "Min", max = "Max")
Response-shape recovery

How well an approach reproduces the shape of the human response distribution, not only its mean. Using the preregistered compare_distributions() in the shared control condition, we score the overlap coefficient (OVL, 1 = identical densities), the Wasserstein-1 distance (W1, 0 = identical, computed as the integral of the absolute ECDF difference, in scale points), the Kolmogorov–Smirnov distance (KS, 0 = indistinguishable), and the variance ratio (clone/human, 1 = matched spread) for each continuous outcome. W1 is the field’s current standard distributional metric for bounded ordinal scales and is added here as the bandwidth-free companion to OVL. The KDE-based OVL depends on kernel bandwidths, which scale with the two samples’ sizes (a large synthetic sample is smoothed differently from the human half) and leak density past the 0/100 scale bounds. W1 has no tuning parameter and reads directly in scale points. The variance ratio stays the headline diagnostic (directional expectation: under-dispersion, ratio < 1). These are inherently per-outcome, control-condition quantities. Figure 4 therefore shows the raw response densities for every continuous outcome, one facet per outcome. Each facet stamps the field’s median variance ratio and Wasserstein-1 distance next to the human replication’s, so spread (VR) and overall distributional distance (W1) can be read at a glance for both the synthetic field and the human reference. All four metrics per outcome are in Table 8.

Code 
dist_per_outcome <- function(pred_data) {
  map_dfr(outcomes_continuous, function(out) {
    compare_distributions(human_data_1, pred_data, out, control_lab,
                          include_w1 = TRUE) |>
      mutate(outcome = out)
  })
}

dist_labels <- c(ovl = "Overlap (OVL)", w1 = "Wasserstein-1",
                 ks_d = "KS distance", variance_ratio = "Variance ratio")

# Per-outcome shape metrics built once per submission, reused by the
# distribution figure and its companion table (cached: 29 x 11 KDE/ECDF
# passes over full respondent-level data).
dist_per_outcome_list <- cached("amendment_dist_per_outcome", {
  map(subgroup_inputs, dist_per_outcome)
})
Code 
# Per facet, stamp the field's median variance ratio and Wasserstein-1 distance
# alongside the human replication's (Human 2 vs Human 1), so each panel shows
# both spread (VR) and overall distributional distance (W1) for the synthetic
# field and the human reference.
field_dist_ann <- imap_dfr(dist_per_outcome_list,
                           ~ mutate(.x, submission = .y)) |>
  filter(submission != "Human replication") |>
  group_by(outcome) |>
  summarise(vr = median(variance_ratio, na.rm = TRUE),
            w1 = median(w1, na.rm = TRUE), .groups = "drop")

human_dist_ann <- dist_per_outcome_list[["Human replication"]] |>
  select(outcome, w1, vr = variance_ratio)

dist_ann <- left_join(field_dist_ann, human_dist_ann,
                      by = "outcome", suffix = c("_field", "_human")) |>
  mutate(label = sprintf("Field   VR %.2f   W1 %.2f\nHuman  VR %.2f   W1 %.2f",
                         vr_field, w1_field, vr_human, w1_human)) |>
  select(outcome, label)

# wrapped primary-outcome label: the full one clips its facet strip
density_labels <- outcome_label_map
density_labels["trust_multidimensional"] <- "Multidimensional trust\n(primary)"

plot_density_overlay(
  human_data_1, human_data_2, llm_data_teams,
  outcomes       = outcomes_continuous,
  condition_val  = control_lab,
  outcome_labels = density_labels,
  annotations    = dist_ann,
  x_lab          = "Response (0–100, control condition)"
)
Code 
field_distribution <- imap_dfr(dist_per_outcome_list, function(d, sub) {
  d |>
    pivot_longer(c(ovl, w1, ks_d, variance_ratio),
                 names_to = "metric", values_to = "value") |>
    mutate(submission = sub)
}) |>
  mutate(outcome = factor(unname(outcome_label_map[outcome]),
                          levels = unname(outcome_label_map))) |>
  relabel_metrics(dist_labels)

field_group_table(field_distribution, "outcome", pct_metrics = character()) |>
  gt() |>
  sub_missing(missing_text = "—") |>
  cols_label(outcome = "Outcome")

Finding 2 — Ranking approaches (secondary)

Having characterised the field, we rank individual approaches. This is the secondary, structural read: each metric is its own ranking, and the interesting result is which approaches lead on which rung. The leaderboard table is the main summary. The ranking visual for the key metric, with its cluster-bootstrap uncertainty intervals, is panel B of Figure 1.

Code 
leaderboard |>
  select(submission, tier, disclosure, directional_pct, spearman_rho, pearson_r,
         pearson_adj, pearson_fz, inferential_pct, rmse, rmse_adj, tost_pct,
         beta, calib_r2, pi_coverage_pct, interval_score) |>
  gt() |>
  fmt_number(columns = c(spearman_rho, pearson_r, pearson_adj, pearson_fz,
                         rmse, rmse_adj, beta, calib_r2, interval_score),
             decimals = 2) |>
  fmt_number(columns = c(directional_pct, inferential_pct, tost_pct,
                         pi_coverage_pct), decimals = 0) |>
  sub_missing(missing_text = "—") |>
  cols_label(
    submission      = "Submission",
    tier            = "Tier",
    disclosure      = "Class",
    directional_pct = "Directional %",
    spearman_rho    = "Spearman ρ",
    pearson_r       = "Pearson r",
    pearson_adj     = "Pearson r (adj.)",
    pearson_fz      = "Pearson r (per-outcome)",
    inferential_pct = "Inferential %",
    rmse            = "RMSE (pp)",
    rmse_adj        = "RMSE (adj., pp)",
    tost_pct        = "TOST %",
    beta            = "Calib. β",
    calib_r2        = "Calib. R²",
    pi_coverage_pct = "PI coverage %",
    interval_score  = "Interval score"
  ) |>
  tab_footnote(
    footnote = "Fisher-z average of the per-outcome correlations. Divergence from the pooled Pearson r flags a submission that predicts which outcomes move without ranking the interventions within them.",
    locations = cells_column_labels(columns = pearson_fz)
  ) |>
  tab_footnote(
    footnote = "Tiers 2–3 only: coverage of the submitted 95% uncertainty intervals (honest intervals land near 95) and the mean Winkler interval score on the 0–100 outcomes (lower = better; a proper score, so neither padding intervals wide nor squeezing them tight pays).",
    locations = cells_column_labels(columns = pi_coverage_pct)
  ) |>
  tab_footnote(
    footnote = "The floor's directional % reads 0, not 50: sign(0) matches neither sign — an artifact of the all-zero prediction, not below-chance skill.",
    locations = cells_body(columns = directional_pct,
                           rows = submission %in% floor_labels)
  ) |>
  tab_style(
    style     = cell_fill(color = "#eef3f7"),
    locations = cells_body(rows = submission == "Human replication")
  ) |>
  tab_style(
    style     = cell_fill(color = "#f4f4f4"),
    locations = cells_body(rows = submission %in% floor_labels)
  )
Cross-metric consistency

A single-metric verdict can mislead: an approach that leads on one rung may trail on another, and Cummins (2025) show empirically that performance on one data feature routinely fails to transfer to others. Rather than leave that to a reader’s cross-panel comparison, we report it directly: the Spearman correlation between the approaches’ rankings on each pair of ladder rungs (reference and floor rows excluded). High values mean the rungs sort the field similarly, so a summary rank would be defensible. Low or negative values mean each rung tells its own story and no single number should be trusted alone.

Code 
metric_cols <- names(ladder_labels)

rank_consistency <- leaderboard |>
  filter(submission != "Human replication", !submission %in% floor_labels) |>
  select(all_of(metric_cols)) |>
  cor(method = "spearman", use = "pairwise.complete.obs")

rank_consistency |>
  as_tibble(rownames = "metric") |>
  mutate(metric = unname(ladder_labels[metric])) |>
  rename_with(~ unname(ladder_labels[.x]), all_of(metric_cols)) |>
  gt() |>
  fmt_number(columns = -metric, decimals = 2) |>
  sub_missing(missing_text = "—") |>
  cols_label(metric = "")

Design analysis: what the metrics can resolve at this sample size

Before locking the metric ladder, we check whether each rung has dynamic range at this design: whether a genuinely good submission can score visibly better than the floor. The check uses design constants and literature priors only and touches no data. The inputs are the design’s cell sizes (≈500 per intervention and ≈1,000 in control per human half), a typical 0–100 slider SD of ≈25 points, and plausible spreads of true megastudy ATEs (SDs of roughly 0.5–2 points: large message-based megastudies rarely move attitude scales by more). Three conclusions follow. First, the reference half-sample ATE SE is ≈1.4 points, the noise floor under every raw metric. Second, we can compute the expected human-replication correlation and the expected correlation of a perfect, noiseless predictor for each plausible true-ATE spread. The perfect predictor’s is the square root of the replication row’s. When the true spread is small relative to 1.4, the radj NA-guard is expected to bind for many entries. Third, v1’s relative TOST bound Δ = 0.5 × |ATEh| (≈0.25–1 point) is smaller than the smallest difference the test could ever confirm (1.645 × sediff ≈ 2.3 points even for a noiseless submission), so that rung would read ~0% for every entry including the human replication row. That is a dead metric. The preregistered fixed bound of 3 scale points (3% of range) gives a perfect predictor roughly 40% per-pair equivalence power while remaining unreachable for badly wrong predictions: dynamic range restored, at the cost of a laxer notion of equivalence, which we accept and state.

Code 
sd_y     <- 25
n_arm_h  <- 500
n_ctrl_h <- 1000
se_ate_h <- sd_y * sqrt(1 / n_arm_h + 1 / n_ctrl_h)
z05      <- qnorm(0.95)

tost_power <- function(delta, se) {
  margin <- delta - z05 * se
  if (margin <= 0) 0 else pnorm(margin / se) - pnorm(-margin / se)
}

tibble(tau = c(0.5, 1, 2)) |>
  mutate(
    se_reference    = se_ate_h,
    r_replication   = tau^2 / (tau^2 + se_ate_h^2),
    r_perfect       = sqrt(r_replication),
    tost_relative   = map_dbl(0.5 * tau, tost_power, se = se_ate_h),
    tost_fixed_3    = tost_power(3, se_ate_h)
  ) |>
  gt() |>
  fmt_number(columns = -tau, decimals = 2) |>
  cols_label(
    tau           = "SD of true ATEs (pts)",
    se_reference  = "Reference SE (pts)",
    r_replication = "r (replication)",
    r_perfect     = "r (perfect predictor)",
    tost_relative = "TOST power, relative Δ",
    tost_fixed_3  = "TOST power, fixed Δ = 3"
  )

Robustness of the scoring pipeline: split-seed sensitivity

The scoring pipeline has researcher degrees of freedom of its own: chiefly the preregistered random seed that splits the human sample into Human 1 and Human 2, and secondarily the TOST bound. Cummins (2025) show that such analytic choices can move fidelity conclusions substantially, and a skeptic could reasonably ask whether the reported reference value reflects the split we happened to draw. We therefore report a resplit sensitivity check: the half-split is redrawn many times with different seeds, the human replication reference is recomputed on each, and the spread of the reference’s metrics across resplits is reported alongside the primary (preregistered-seed) values. A narrow spread demonstrates that the seed is not driving the results. When real data arrive, the same check is run for the full field: every submission is rescored against each resplit’s Human 1, and the stability of the field summary (and of leaderboard ranks) across resplits is reported.

Code 
resplit_ceiling <- cached("amendment_resplit_ceiling", {
  all_ids <- unique(human_data$id)

  map_dfr(1:50, function(i) {
    set.seed(1000 + i)
    ids_i <- sample(all_ids, size = floor(length(all_ids) / 2))
    h1_i  <- human_data |> filter(id %in% ids_i)
    h2_i  <- human_data |> filter(!id %in% ids_i)

    side_of <- function(dat, suffix) {
      map_dfr(outcomes_continuous, function(out) {
        prep_ate(run_main_treatment_model(dat, outcome = out)) |>
          mutate(outcome = out)
      }) |>
        rename_with(~ paste0(.x, suffix), c(estimate, se, p_adj))
    }

    inner_join(side_of(h1_i, "_h"), side_of(h2_i, "_l"),
               by = c("condition", "outcome")) |>
      mutate(infer_l = classify_sig(estimate_l, p_adj_l)) |>
      # continuous outcomes only, so raw units are already pp of scale range
      pooled_metrics(include_rmse = TRUE) |>
      mutate(resplit = i)
  })
})

resplit_ceiling |>
  pivot_longer(-resplit, names_to = "metric", values_to = "value") |>
  filter(metric %in% names(ladder_labels)) |>
  group_by(metric) |>
  summarise(mean = mean(value, na.rm = TRUE), sd = sd(value, na.rm = TRUE),
            min = min(value, na.rm = TRUE),  max = max(value, na.rm = TRUE),
            n_defined = sum(!is.na(value)), .groups = "drop") |>
  left_join(
    # Restricted to the continuous outcomes for comparability with the
    # resplits (whose side_of() maps outcomes_continuous only).
    pairs_list[["Human replication"]] |>
      filter(outcome %in% outcomes_continuous) |>
      pooled_metrics(include_rmse = TRUE) |>
      pivot_longer(everything(), names_to = "metric",
                   values_to = "preregistered_seed"),
    by = "metric"
  ) |>
  mutate(metric = factor(unname(ladder_labels[metric]),
                         levels = unname(ladder_labels))) |>
  arrange(metric) |>
  gt() |>
  fmt_number(columns = c(mean, sd, min, max, preregistered_seed), decimals = 2) |>
  sub_missing(missing_text = "—") |>
  cols_label(metric = "Metric", mean = "Mean", sd = "SD", min = "Min",
             max = "Max", n_defined = "N defined",
             preregistered_seed = "Preregistered seed")

When real submissions arrive, both findings are recomputed by replacing the placeholder datasets with the submitted prediction files routed through their tier’s builder (build_pairs_t1() / build_pairs_t2() / build_pairs_t3()). The subgroup and distribution figures are produced for the tiers eligible for them, alongside the demographic-baseline and stereotyping diagnostics of Section 3, including the demographic parity gap (demographic_parity_gap()). The approach-characteristics table coded from the registration forms is then joined to the leaderboard for the exploratory analysis of which design choices accompany better predictions (publicly disclosed features only, with Class C entries excluded from this analysis, not from the leaderboard).

References

Arruda, Henrique Ferraz de, Carlos Gracia Lázaro, Alberto Aleta, and Yamir Moreno. 2026. “Collective Cooperation Without Individual Fidelity in LLM Agents.” arXiv. https://doi.org/10.48550/arXiv.2606.30454.
Ashokkumar, Ashwini, Luke Hewitt, Isaias Ghezae, and Robb Willer. 2026. “Large Language Models Can Predict the Results of Social Science Experiments.” Nature, July, 1–8. https://doi.org/10.1038/s41586-026-10742-x.
Cummins, Jamie. 2025. “The Threat of Analytic Flexibility in Using Large Language Models to Simulate Human Data.” arXiv. https://doi.org/10.48550/ARXIV.2509.13397.
Feuerriegel, Stefan, Christopher Barrie, M. J. Crockett, Laura K. Globig, Killian L. McLoughlin, Dan-Mircea Mirea, Arthur Spirling, et al. 2026. “A Reporting Checklist for Large Language Models in Behavioural Science.” Nature Human Behaviour, June, 1–5. https://doi.org/10.1038/s41562-026-02492-7.
Park, Joon Sung, Carolyn Q. Zou, Jonne Kamphorst, Niles Egan, Aaron Shaw, Benjamin Mako Hill, Carrie Cai, et al. 2026. “LLM Agents Grounded in Self-Reports Enable General-Purpose Simulation of Individuals.” arXiv. https://doi.org/10.48550/arXiv.2411.10109.

Footnotes

  1. The original experiment contains 20 conditions. The benchmark scores only the 16 non-interactive text interventions. The four left out are the three interactive LLM-chatbot arms and the “Value similarity” quiz, all of which depend on a live back-and-forth that would substantially complicate the simulation task.↩︎

  2. We take Ashokkumar et al.’s megastudy figures, not their headline r = .85, as the reference because the two numbers measure different tasks. The .85 is a correlation over 469 effects pooled from 70 unrelated survey experiments, spanning many topics, treatments, and outcomes. Their megastudy figures instead measure how well the model distinguishes the several interventions of a single megastudy against that study’s shared control, which is exactly this benchmark’s setting (16 interventions, one control). Ashokkumar et al. report the megastudies as a separate secondary archive (15 megastudies, 606 effects) and note that these correlations run lower than the primary archive’s.↩︎

Source Code
---
title: "The Silicon Sample Benchmark: A multi-team preregistration for predicting behavioral-experiment effects"

abstract: |
    This is the preregistration for the **Silicon Sample Benchmark**, a multi-team prediction benchmark. We invite independent research teams to predict the results of a novel behavioral megastudy (16 message interventions to increase trust in climate scientists, 13 preregistered outcomes, a representative U.S. sample of ~18,000) using any AI-based approach they choose—behavioral clones, group-level simulation, or direct effect forecasting. Submissions enter at one of **three tiers** defined by the granularity of the prediction (individual-level synthetic data, cell-level statistics, or effect-level estimates with uncertainty intervals), and every approach is scored on the same preregistered metric ladder against the same human replication reference. This document is **self-contained**: it restates the full analysis plan so that a participating team never needs to consult the project's earlier, single-team preregistration (v1); the section *About this preregistration* narrates what it keeps from v1 and what it changes. At the time of writing, human data are partially collected (~10,000 of the ~18,000 responses); none of these data have been shared, published, or made accessible to any prospective team, and no team will be granted access before the prediction lock. Before any human data are released, every team registers its approach using a standardized method-registration form (an extended version of the GUIDE-LLM reporting checklist) and deposits its predictions on Zenodo—publicly, or partly escrowed under a disclosure policy that keeps every entry locked and verifiable even where it cannot be fully public.

date: "2026-06-11"

format:
  html:
    code-fold: true
    toc: true
    toc-depth: 4
  # PDF disabled for now to speed up iteration — re-enable for the final render.
  # pdf:
  #   toc: true
  #   toc-depth: 5
  #   include-before-body:
  #     text: |
  #       \clearpage
  #   colorlinks: true
  #   tbl-colwidths: true
  #   header-includes:
  #     - \usepackage{float}
  #     - \floatplacement{figure}{H}
  #     - \floatplacement{table}{H}
  #     - \usepackage{listings}
  #     - \lstset{breaklines=true, basicstyle=\small\ttfamily}

execute:
  echo: true
  warning: false
  message: false
  fig-width: 8
  fig-height: 5
  fig-dpi: 300
---

```{r}
#| label: setup
#| output: false

# packages
library(tidyverse)
library(sandwich)
library(lmtest)
library(marginaleffects)
library(broom)
library(MetBrewer)
library(gghalves)
library(patchwork)
library(gt)
library(tinytable)
library(here)

# custom functions (reused unchanged from the original preregistration)
source(here("R/functions/statistics.R"))
source(here("R/functions/plots.R"))
source(here("R/functions/tables.R"))
source(here("R/functions/reporting.R"))

# simulated (random) placeholder data
human_data     <- readRDS(here("data/simulation_preregistration/human_data_preregistration.rds"))
llm_data_teams <- readRDS(here("data/simulation_preregistration/llm_data_preregistration_teams.rds"))

# Restrict the human data to the scored conditions (the 16 non-interactive
# interventions + control) BEFORE any model is fit. The real cleaned human
# data will additionally contain the three chatbot arms and the value-
# similarity arm, which no submission predicts; fitting the human models on
# those arms would put the human side's BH adjustment over a different test
# family than the submissions'. A no-op on the placeholder data (which has
# scored conditions only), load-bearing when the real loader is wired in.
scored_conditions <- levels(droplevels(factor(llm_data_teams$condition)))
human_data <- human_data |>
  filter(condition %in% scored_conditions) |>
  mutate(condition = droplevels(condition))

# Same preregistered random split of the human sample as in the original
# preregistration. Human 1 is the reference half all submissions are scored
# against; Human 2 is the human replication reference.
set.seed(42)
split_ids    <- sample(unique(human_data$id), size = floor(length(unique(human_data$id)) / 2))
human_data_1 <- human_data |> filter(id %in% split_ids)
human_data_2 <- human_data |> filter(!id %in% split_ids)
```


::: {.callout-warning}
