**This is an ongoing draft, to share with collaborators. This preregistration is not yet registered and will change still.**
:::

::: {.callout-important}
**This is the canonical preregistration for the benchmark.** It is self-contained: the full analysis plan is restated here (see *Preregistered analysis plan*), so a participating team does not need to open the project's earlier single-team preregistration ([v1](preregistration.qmd)). The section *About this preregistration* explains why this second preregistration exists, what it keeps from v1, and every way it changes.

The benchmark's public-facing design (motivation, rules, the three tiers, the full method-registration form, the disclosure policy, scoring, and the timeline) is also described in the [call for participation](index.html). To avoid duplication, this document summarizes that design only as far as the analysis needs it, and concentrates on the **preregistered analysis plan**. Where the two overlap, the call is the canonical description of the procedure and this document is the canonical description of the analysis.

All datasets used for demonstration here are simulated random noise with the correct structure.
:::

## About this preregistration

**Why a second preregistration.** The project began as a single-team simulation study, preregistered as *"Predicting the effects of behavioral interventions on humans with LLMs"* (the original preregistration, **v1**). That plan asked how well a single approach, a group-level simulation built by the core team from demographically representative U.S. profiles with a particular model and prompt, recovers a novel megastudy. But a single team's result is only one point in a large design space of ways to build a synthetic sample. This document reconceives the project as a **multi-team benchmark**: many independent teams predict the same novel experiment with any AI-based approach, and every approach is scored identically. It is now the **canonical, self-contained** plan for the project, superseding the original **v1** preregistration.

**What it keeps from v1.** The scientific target and the analytical backbone are unchanged. The estimands (average treatment effects per intervention × outcome, subgroup interactions, control-condition response distributions, and demographic baselines), the three-section analysis structure, the least-to-most-strict metric ladder, and the preregistered split-half **human replication reference** are all carried over from v1 and **restated in full below** (see *Preregistered analysis plan*) rather than referenced by pointer. The model and comparison functions in `R/functions/statistics.R` are reused unchanged.

**What it changes and adds.** This preregistration is written for teams who are **blinded to the human outcome data**. Revising the analysis plan therefore does not carry the usual risk of data-contingent researcher degrees of freedom. The changes fall into three groups: how the study is reconceived, what it now reports, and a set of scoring add-ons drawn from the recent silicon-sample literature. Each is summarized here at a high level, with a pointer to the section that specifies it in full.

**A. From one approach to a field.** These change the *design*. They leave v1's analysis pipeline untouched.

1. **Single team → multi-team benchmark.** The project now scores a *field* of independent submissions rather than one core-team approach, through three submission tiers defined by prediction granularity (Tier 1 individual-level synthetic data, Tier 2 cell-level statistics, Tier 3 effect-level estimates with uncertainty intervals). See *Benchmark design*.
2. **Method registration and disclosure.** Every team documents its pipeline on a standardized method-registration form (an extended GUIDE-LLM checklist) and deposits its predictions under a disclosure policy that keeps even proprietary entries verifiable. See *Method registration and disclosure*.
3. **Per-tier submission specifications.** Each tier has an exact file schema, and the shared estimands are recovered from whatever a tier supplies. For instance, effect standard errors are reconstructed from the uncertainty intervals that Tiers 2–3 submit. See *Submission specification* and *Tier-specific estimand recovery*.

**B. What the benchmark reports.** These sit on top of v1's pipeline. No estimand, model, or metric inherited from v1 is redefined.

4. **The headline result is the field, not a single scorecard.** The primary result is no longer one approach's report card but how the *whole field* of approaches fares at predicting human behavior. The main purpose is to present the distribution of predictive quality across the field. Every approach is scored against a split-half **human replication reference** (how well one half of the human sample predicts the other). Teams may enter more than once, so the field summary counts one primary entry per team. A team may also choose to predict only part of the study rather than every intervention and outcome. The main leaderboard is therefore limited to *full-coverage* entries (those that predicted the complete set), so its scores are all measured on the same basis. Partial entries are reported separately. See *Cross-team comparison*.
5. **One score per metric, pooled across outcomes.** A single data point is one predicted effect placed next to the matching human effect, for example how much a particular message shifted donations to the science organization. For each metric on the ladder, all such comparisons (every intervention paired with every outcome, each expressed in percentage points of its outcome's scale range — see item 16) are pooled into one number, which uses all of them at once rather than scoring each outcome on its own. We deliberately do *not* collapse the metrics into one overall "champion" score. An approach can get the direction of effects right while mis-scaling their size, or do well on the main outcome and badly on the rest, and a single number would hide exactly the structure the benchmark exists to reveal. Each metric is therefore read on its own. See *Cross-team comparison* (*Scoring principle*).
6. **An uncertainty interval on every score.** The benchmark tests 16 interventions, but these are only a sample of the messages one could have tested. A different study could have used 16 others of the same kind. An approach's score is therefore an estimate, and like any estimate it would move somewhat under a different draw of interventions. We attach an interval to every score showing how much it would move, so that a small gap between two approaches is not mistaken for a real difference when it sits within that wobble. For the same reason, we never call one approach better than another from the ranking alone: the claim has to pass a preregistered test that compares the two directly. See *Uncertainty intervals on the leaderboard*.

**C. Evaluation add-ons from the silicon-sample literature.** The remaining additions extend *how* an approach is scored, each motivated by how recent work validates synthetic samples. Every one is new to this preregistration, supplements rather than replaces an inherited metric, and points to the section where it is specified in full.

7. **A noise-corrected correlation.** The human effects an approach is scored against are themselves estimated from a finite (here, half-split) sample, so they carry sampling noise that mechanically drags every correlation down. This penalizes all approaches, and the reference itself, for something unrelated to predictive skill. Following @ashokkumarLargeLanguageModels2026a, we report a noise-corrected correlation *beside* the raw one (the raw value stays the ranked metric). See *Comparison functions*, and *Section 2*, where small demographic cells make the correction matter most.
8. **Did it pick the right interventions?** The correlation rungs summarize the whole effect pattern, but the decision a many-arm megastudy exists to inform is narrower: *which* interventions are worth acting on. We score that directly: are an approach's top-ranked interventions the ones with the largest true effects [@ashokkumarLargeLanguageModels2026a]? See *Section 1*.
9. **A floor for the rungs without a natural zero.** Some rungs (inferential agreement, equivalence, and RMSE) have no natural zero to read them against, and on inferential agreement a lazy "predict no effect for everything" strategy scores deceptively well when most true effects are small and not statistically significant. Adding a naive "do-nothing" approach as a floor reference makes these scores interpretable. Each approach is then read as the distance between that floor and the human reference [@ashokkumarLargeLanguageModels2026a; @cumminsThreatAnalyticFlexibility2025b]. See *Comparison functions*.
10. **A fixed bound for the equivalence test.** One rung asks whether a predicted effect is *close enough* to the human effect to count as equivalent, which needs a bound for "close enough." v1 set that bound as a fraction of the human effect being tested. But that effect is itself measured with error, so the bound moved with the noise and the test was not valid. We now fix the bound in advance, at a set fraction of each outcome's scale, so it no longer depends on the numbers under test. See *Comparison functions*.
11. **Is an approach consistent across metrics?** Cummins' central finding is that a configuration strong on one measure is routinely weak on another, so any single-metric verdict misleads. We report how stable each approach's rank is across the whole ladder [@cumminsThreatAnalyticFlexibility2025b]. See *Cross-team comparison*.
12. **A fairness gap, and under-dispersion foregrounded.** Averaging baseline accuracy over demographic groups can hide an approach that serves most groups well but consistently misses one, so we report the gap between the worst- and best-served group [@parkLLMAgentsGrounded2026]. Because too-similar synthetic responses (under-dispersion) are the failure mode the literature flags most often, we make under-dispersion the headline distribution diagnostic. See *Section 3*, and *Section 1* (response distributions).
13. **Does the result depend on an arbitrary split?** The human sample is divided in half at random to build the reference. That split is fixed before any data are seen, so it is not a researcher degree of freedom. But the particular split we drew is still arbitrary, and another random split would serve just as well. To show the results do not hinge on the one we happened to draw, we recompute them across many alternative splits and report how stable they are [@cumminsThreatAnalyticFlexibility2025b]. See *Cross-team comparison*.
14. **A stated scope: populations, not individuals.** The benchmark cannot assess individual-level fidelity, because teams build their own synthetic respondents with no pairing to specific humans. Its distribution metrics are therefore claims about the *population* response shape, not about matching any person. We say so plainly, because aggregate agreement can coexist with wrong individual behavior [@parkLLMAgentsGrounded2026; @arrudaCollectiveCooperationIndividual2026]. See *Overview* (scope statement).
15. **Scoring the uncertainty intervals themselves.** Teams in Tiers 2–3 submit not only a predicted effect but an uncertainty interval around it, and we use that interval to decide whether the effect counts as statistically significant (does the interval exclude zero?). This opens a loophole: a team that submits very wide intervals will have every interval include zero, so every prediction reads "not significant". Since most effects in behavioral experiments are expected to be relatively small, matching "not significant" everywhere might earn a high agreement score with no real skill, just by being vague. To close the loophole we also score the intervals directly: whether they are well-calibrated (containing the true effect about as often as they claim to) and appropriately narrow. Being vague then carries its own cost. See *Tier-specific estimand recovery*.
16. **One unit for all pooled scores.** The outcomes sit on different scales: 0–100 attitude sliders, a $0–10 donation, a 0–1 signup probability. Pooling effects in their raw units would let the sliders drown out the two behavioral outcomes (whose effects are numerically 10–100 times smaller) and would hand every submission a cluster of free near-zero points. Every estimate is therefore converted to **percentage points of its outcome's scale range** before any cross-outcome scoring, following the practice of @ashokkumarLargeLanguageModels2026a, who pool effects only in these units and never across raw scales. With one common unit, RMSE also becomes comparable across outcomes and is reported pooled (it previously could not be). The binary newsletter outcome is added to the subgroup analysis on the same footing, via the linear probability model. This changes preregistered scoring quantities, which is why it is made now, before any team registers. See *Cross-team comparison* (*Scoring principle*) and *Section 2*.

Everything in groups B and C is **additive**: each item adds to an inherited metric without removing or redefining one. Two things do change from v1, both only for the cross-team comparison and both explained above. First, the equivalence test now uses a bound fixed in advance (item 10). Second, we keep v1's split-half human comparison exactly as it was and change only its name: v1 called it a "ceiling," which wrongly implies the best any approach could reach, so we rename it the human replication reference (item 4).


## Motivation

Whether large language models can stand in for human participants is a consequential open question in behavioral science, and the evidence so far is mixed and hard to interpret. Most existing evaluations target experiments that were already published, and plausibly in the models' training data. Almost none are preregistered, so reported successes are entangled with researcher degrees of freedom in model and prompt choice [@cumminsThreatAnalyticFlexibility2025b].

We therefore aim to provide a preregistered, multi-team prediction benchmark, the *Silicon Sample Benchmark*: independent teams predict the same novel megastudy (N ≈ 18,000) with any AI-based approach, and every approach is scored on the same preregistered metric ladder. The design addresses both problems: (i) the target is novel (none of the interventions has been tested in this form), and (ii) every prediction is deposited and time-stamped before any human data are released. The full rationale, rules, and incentives for participating teams are laid out in the [call for participation](index.html).

The benchmark scores the 16 non-interactive text interventions of the megastudy^[The original experiment contains 20 conditions. The benchmark scores only the 16 non-interactive text interventions. The four left out are the three interactive LLM-chatbot arms and the "Value similarity" quiz, all of which depend on a live back-and-forth that would substantially complicate the simulation task.], across self-reported attitudes (e.g., trust in climate scientists, support for funding of climate science) and behavioral measures (a donation to a scientific organization, a newsletter sign-up).

@tbl-interventions gives an overview of the 16 interventions, grouped by their content. All are short text messages (e.g., an article, an interview excerpt, corrected statistics, a portrait) shown once before the outcome battery. Together with the shared control condition they make up the 17 conditions every submission needs to cover.

```{r}
#| label: tbl-interventions
#| tbl-cap: "The 16 text-based interventions scored in the benchmark, grouped by the broad angle each takes on trust in climate scientists. The **Code name** column gives the internal condition identifier used in the raw survey files (`survey.qsf` / `survey_definition.json`), for teams that parse those directly. Each intervention has exactly one code name; where intervention-author teams were combined, the code name is a semicolon-joined list of animal pairs, and the semicolons are part of the name — match on the full string, never split it (see `survey/condition_codenames.csv` in the benchmark repository). The original megastudy also fields three interactive LLM-chatbot arms and a 'Value similarity' quiz; these four are interactive and excluded from the simulation benchmark, leaving these 16 plus a shared control condition (17 conditions in total)."

interventions <- read_csv(here("data/interventions.csv"), show_col_types = FALSE)

tag_order <- c(
  "Collaboration and peer-review",
  "Scientific methods and results",
  "Applications and impact",
  "Others' endorsement",
  "Values",
  "Other"
)

# Same table layout as v1: category headers inserted
# as bold full-width rows, with a running intervention number.
intervention_table_data <- interventions |>
  mutate(across(c(title, summary, tag, code_name), str_trim)) |>
  filter(tag != "LLM-chatbot", title != "Value similarity") |>
  mutate(tag = factor(tag, levels = tag_order)) |>
  arrange(tag, title) |>
  mutate(number = as.character(row_number())) |>
  # internal condition code names exactly as in the raw survey files
  # (survey.qsf / survey_definition.json); semicolon-joined names are a
  # single identifier — semicolons are part of the name, never split.
  mutate(codename = code_name) |>
  group_by(tag) |>
  group_modify(~ bind_rows(
    tibble(title = as.character(.y$tag), codename = "", summary = ""),
    .x
  )) |>
  ungroup() |>
  mutate(` ` = ifelse(title %in% levels(tag), "", number)) |>
  select(` `, title, codename, summary) |>
  rename(`Intervention Title` = title, `Code name` = codename, Summary = summary)

# identify header rows
header_rows <- which(intervention_table_data$` ` == "")

intervention_table_data |>
  tt(width = c(0.04, 0.19, 0.17, 0.60)) |>
  format_tt(escape = TRUE) |>
  style_tt(align = "l", alignv = "t") |>
  style_tt(i = 0, bold = TRUE) |>
  style_tt(i = header_rows, bold = TRUE, j = 2, colspan = 3) |>
  style_tt(j = 3, monospace = TRUE) |>
  style_tt(fontsize = 0.85)
```

The first goal of the benchmark is *not* to crown a single champion team. It is to map the current design space of synthetic sample approaches and to evaluate, as a field, how well they predict human behavior. This benchmark study will provide a **catalogue**: a structured, comparable description of how research teams currently approach AI prediction of behavioral experiments (open- vs. closed-weight models, persona sources, administration protocols, fine-tuning, in-context learning) and which of these choices appear to matter. We will evaluate approaches on a ladder of metrics of increasing strictness, from directional agreement to formal equivalence, plus distributional fidelity and stereotyping diagnostics (all specified in full under *Preregistered analysis plan*, below). We expect different approaches to win on different rungs.

## The human target sample

Predictions are scored against the human data from the megastudy itself. Participants are recruited from a non-probability opt-in panel, quota-matched to the population of U.S. residents, all aged 18 or older, who pass a series of attention and bot-detection checks before treatment assignment.

The benchmark targets **N ≈ 18,000 complete responses**: 1,000 per intervention across the 16 text-based interventions, plus 2,000 in the shared control condition. Recruitment uses **census-based cross quotas** on gender × age and gender × race/ethnicity to approximate the U.S. adult population. The targets below match the proportions of the parent megastudy, with counts rescaled to a total N ≈ 18,000. Participants selecting "Other" as their gender are not quota-constrained.

The targets below are drawn from the 2024 U.S. Census Bureau Population Estimates Program. *Total* is the share within each category. *Male* and *Female* give the within-category gender split. The table describes the key demographics of the human sample. Participating teams are not required to use this information for their own synthetic sample.

```{r}
#| label: tbl-quotas
#| tbl-cap: "Sampling quota targets (N ≈ 18,000), from the 2024 U.S. Census Bureau Population Estimates Program. Proportions match the parent megastudy, with counts rescaled to N ≈ 18,000, while the target of the megastudy is N≈ 22,000."

# Same layout as the interventions table: category headers inserted as bold
# full-width rows above their levels (tinytable, for a consistent look).
quotas <- read_csv(here("data/quotas_18000.csv"), show_col_types = FALSE)

quota_table_data <- quotas |>
  group_by(Variable) |>
  group_modify(~ bind_rows(
    tibble(Category = as.character(.y$Variable), Total = "", Male = "", Female = ""),
    .x
  )) |>
  ungroup() |>
  select(Category, Total, Male, Female) |>
  rename(` ` = Category)

header_rows_q <- which(quota_table_data$Total == "")

quota_table_data |>
  tt(width = c(0.40, 0.20, 0.20, 0.20)) |>
  format_tt(escape = TRUE) |>
  style_tt(align = "l", alignv = "t") |>
  style_tt(i = 0, bold = TRUE) |>
  style_tt(i = header_rows_q, bold = TRUE) |>
  style_tt(fontsize = 0.85)
```

Gender, age, and race are the only mandatory survey items (needed to fill the quotas) and so enter every analysis model as covariates with no missing data. The six demographic moderators used for the subgroup and calibration analyses (gender, age, race, education, income, and partisan identity) are specified under *Preregistered analysis plan* (below) and in the codebook. Full sampling and exclusion detail is in the [human study preregistration](https://doi.org/10.5281/zenodo.20160212).

## Benchmark design

Participation is by invitation. Each team builds an AI-based prediction pipeline from the shipped materials (intervention texts, the full survey instrument, the codebook, the registration template, and the validator). 

Teams can use any models, personas, prompts, fine-tuning, retrieval, or prior literature they want. They will receive **no participant pool** (each team builds its own profiles) and **never any human outcome data**. Before the deadline for the prediction lock, each team registers its approach by depositing its synthetic sample/predictions and materials on Zenodo, hashed and time-stamped. After the prediction lock deadline (and once the human data collection is finished), every submission runs through the preregistered analysis pipeline unchanged and is reported on a public leaderboard alongside the human replication reference, and in a joint manuscript with all participating teams as co-authors. Three ground rules define a valid entry and matter for the analysis. **Blinding is absolute**: no team accesses, solicits, or is shown any human outcome data before the lock (at registration time only ~10,000 of the planned ~18,000 responses had been collected and none released). Pipelines are **AI-based and automated**. **Everything is registered though not necessarily public** (see the disclosure policy below). The full procedure, ground rules, timeline, and incentives are in the [call for participation](index.html).

**Organizer access and firewall.** Blinding constrains the organizers too, so we state who has seen what. Interim human data (the ~10,000 responses collected at the time of writing) have been accessed only by the research lead, Jan Pfänder, for data-quality monitoring. As a result, members or affiliates of the core team who have had **no access to any human outcome data** may enter the benchmark as regular teams under the same rules as everyone else (same registration form, same blinding attestation, same deadlines). 

For full disclosure, Jan Pfänder presented some very preliminary results (human megastudy sample at the time N ≈ 3000) from an initial simulation attempt (following preregistration **v1**) in a 5min talk at a closed academic workshop at the Max Planck Institute for Human Development in Berlin (May 2026). This is the only presentation that has targeted an audience of potential collaborators on the benchmark study. It focused on the prediction ability of the simulation approach and revealed nothing about how humans behaved in the megastudy. Two other talks were explicitly about the human megastudy results: Jan Pfänder presented preliminary results from the human megastudy at an invited talk at the Max Planck Institute for Human Development in Berlin (May 2026, N at the time ≈ 3000 of 22000), and at a small conference at the University of Hohenheim (June 2026, N at the time ≈ 13000 of 22000). No one in the audience of these talks will be invited to participate in the benchmark study. In addition, since we ask for extensive disclosure of materials, a "cheating" prompt (e.g., "make sure intervention XX does best") would surface in the reporting framework that teams are required to fill out.

### Three ways to compete

We deliberately welcome very different approaches. Submissions enter at one of **three tiers**, defined by the granularity of the prediction. The common currency across tiers is the set of preregistered estimands, the same quantities the human analysis produces, so every approach is scored on the rungs it can reach. Teams choose the highest tier their approach supports. Lower tiers are simply eligible for fewer analyses.

- **Tier 1 · Full: individual-level synthetic data.** A respondent-per-row dataset of behavioral clones: each synthetic participant completes the experiment in one condition. Flows through the preregistered analysis pipeline completely unchanged. Eligible for everything: ATE recovery, calibration regression, response distributions, subgroup heterogeneity, demographic baselines, and stereotyping diagnostics. *Grain: 1 row = 1 synthetic respondent.*
- **Tier 2 · Cell-level: group-level predictions.** A predicted mean and a **95% uncertainty interval** on that mean per cell (per condition × outcome, and per condition × moderator-level × outcome). For approaches that simulate or reason about groups rather than individuals. Eligible for ATEs, calibration, subgroup heterogeneity, and demographic baselines. Not eligible for distribution-shape metrics (OVL, KS) or predictability regressions. *Grain: 1 row = 1 cell (condition × [moderator level ×] outcome).*
- **Tier 3 · Effect-level: direct effect predictions.** One predicted ATE per intervention × outcome, in original outcome units, with a **95% uncertainty interval** expressing the team's epistemic uncertainty. For zero-shot forecasting, in-context learning over the literature, meta-analytic priors, or hybrid pipelines. Eligible for directional agreement, Spearman ρ, Pearson r, RMSE, and calibration regression. Inferential agreement and TOST are computed from the submitted intervals. *Grain: 1 row = 1 intervention × outcome estimate.*

### Analysis eligibility by tier

| Preregistered analysis | Tier 1 | Tier 2 | Tier 3 |
|---|:-:|:-:|:-:|
| ATE recovery — six-metric ladder (Section 1) | ✓ | ✓ | ✓ * |
| Calibration regression α / β (Section 1) | ✓ | ✓ | ✓ |
| Response distributions — variance ratio, OVL, KS (Section 1) | ✓ | — | — |
| Subgroup heterogeneity — condition × moderator (Section 2) | ✓ | ✓ | — |
| Within-subgroup distributions (Section 2) | ✓ | — | — |
| Demographic baseline calibration (Section 3) | ✓ | ✓ | — |
| Demographic predictability / stereotyping (Section 3) | ✓ | — | — |

\* For Tiers 2 and 3, inferential agreement and equivalence (TOST) are evaluated against the submitted 95% uncertainty intervals rather than model-based standard errors. For Tier 1, standard errors fall out of the fitted models, so no separate uncertainty input is required.

## Method registration and disclosure

Before the prediction lock, every team documents its pipeline on a structured **method-registration form** and deposits it alongside the predictions. The form builds on the GUIDE-LLM reporting checklist [@feuerriegelReportingChecklistLarge2026], extended where a reporting checklist alone does not guarantee full replicability of a prediction pipeline. The full item-by-item form and the **disclosure policy** (which lets proprietary teams seal items under escrow while keeping the lock publicly verifiable) are published in the [call for participation](index.html#registration-form). A fillable template ships in the [benchmark repository](https://github.com/janpfander/silicon-sample-submission). Two aspects of the registration feed the analysis directly:

- **Approach characteristics** recorded on the form (open- vs. closed-weight model, persona source, administration protocol, fine-tuning, in-context learning, and so on) are the features joined to the leaderboard for the exploratory design-choice analysis (see *Cross-team comparison*).
- **Disclosure class** is a per-entry badge the policy assigns from the entry's most restricted item: **Class A · Open** (all items public), **Class B · Escrowed** (some items sealed but available to the core team and auditors under confidentiality, so compliance stays verifiable), and **Class C · Sealed** (one or more items withheld even from escrow). All three classes receive full leaderboard standing, carrying their badge. Only publicly disclosed features enter the design-choice analysis, and Class C entries are excluded from that analysis only, not from the leaderboard.

## Submission specification

Each tier defines the exact prediction file(s) a submission provides. The schemas below are the contract the scoring code consumes (`build_pairs_t1()` / `build_pairs_t2()` / `build_pairs_t3()`, see *Cross-team comparison*). The deposit logistics (Zenodo record structure, the `metadata.json` file, file naming, the self-check validator, and the public/escrowed split) are specified on the [materials page](materials.html) and in the [benchmark repository](https://github.com/janpfander/silicon-sample-submission). Two requirements bear directly on the analysis: full **coverage** is all 16 interventions plus control across all 13 outcomes (declared subsets are permitted and scored pairwise, with coverage reported on the leaderboard), and **condition and column labels must match the published codebook exactly** (submissions are validated before the lock, and cells that fail to parse are dropped).

### Tier 1 — individual-level dataset

One row per synthetic respondent, matching the structure of the cleaned analysis data (`cleaned_llm.rds` schema). Each team supplies its own synthetic respondents: `profile_id` is a team-assigned identifier, unique within the submission, and assignment of respondents to conditions (between-subjects, covering all 17 conditions) is the team's responsibility, registered under C.4/C.6.

| Column(s) | Type | Specification |
|---|---|------|
| `profile_id` | string | A unique respondent identifier you assign (unique within the submission). |
| `condition` | factor | Control or one of the 16 intervention labels, exactly as in the codebook. |
| `gender`, `age_band`, `race`, `education`, `income`, `party` | factor | The six moderators, levels per codebook (from your own profiles). |
| `trust_multidimensional` + 12 trust items | numeric | Primary composite 0–100. The 12 underlying items per codebook scale. |
| `trust_post`, `distrust_post`, `funding_perceptions`, `policy_role_mean`, `inst_trust_mean` | numeric | Secondary outcomes, original scales per codebook. |
| `belief_post`, `concern_mean`, `policy_general`, `policy_specific_mean`, `behavior_mean` | numeric | Tertiary outcomes, original scales per codebook. |
| `donation_ams` | numeric | Donation to AMS in $, per codebook. |
| `newsletter_signup` | 0 / 1 | Binary behavioral outcome. |

Because a Tier 1 submission has the same shape as the human participant data, the same analysis code applies. 

**Precision requirement.** An approach's estimated effects are only as precise as the synthetic sample behind them. The fewer synthetic respondents, the noisier each estimated effect, and a very small sample would score poorly for a reason that has nothing to do with the method. A Tier 1 submission must therefore contain at least as many synthetic respondents as the human half it is scored against: **500 per intervention and 1,000 in the control condition**. At that size the submission's effects carry about the same sampling noise as the human reference, so neither side's noise dominates the comparison.

We encourage teams to go well beyond this minimum. A larger synthetic sample gives more precise effect estimates that reflect the method itself rather than the luck of a particular draw, and synthetic respondents are cheap to generate. @ashokkumarLargeLanguageModels2026a took the same approach, averaging many simulated responses per condition to keep their predictions precise.

### Tier 2 — cell-level file(s)

Two long-format files. `*_cells_main.csv`: one row per condition × outcome. `*_cells_moderator.csv`: one row per condition × moderator × moderator-level × outcome.

| Column | Type | Specification |
|---|---|------|
| `condition` | factor | Per codebook. |
| `moderator`, `moderator_level` | factor | Moderator file only. Levels per codebook. |
| `outcome` | string | Outcome variable name per codebook. |
| `mean` | numeric | Predicted cell mean on the original scale (proportion for `newsletter_signup`). |
| `pi_lower`, `pi_upper` | numeric | 95% uncertainty interval bounds on the cell mean: the team's epistemic uncertainty about the *human* cell mean, not a within-simulation SD. The ATE interval is derived by combining the treatment- and control-cell intervals. Used for inferential-agreement and equivalence scoring. |

### Tier 3 — effect-level file

One row per intervention × outcome (16 × 13 = 208 rows for full coverage). Estimates are ATEs versus control on the original outcome scale. The binary outcome is on the probability scale.

| Column | Type | Specification |
|---|---|------|
| `condition` | factor | Intervention label per codebook. |
| `outcome` | string | Outcome variable name per codebook. |
| `ate` | numeric | Predicted ATE, original units. |
| `pi_lower`, `pi_upper` | numeric | 95% uncertainty interval bounds: the team's epistemic uncertainty about the *human* ATE, not a within-simulation standard error. Used for inferential-agreement and equivalence scoring. |

## Preregistered analysis plan

### Overview and the human replication reference

Each submission is scored against the human data with the same three-section plan, restated in full in the subsections below so that this document stands alone. The plan is applied **symmetrically** to the human sample and to every submission: the same model and comparison functions (`R/functions/statistics.R`) run on both, so a submission is never held to a standard the human data is not.

The human sample is split into two halves by a preregistered random seed. **Human 1** is the reference half every submission is scored against. The agreement between **Human 1 and Human 2** defines the **human replication reference**: how closely two independent halves of the human sample agree on the same estimands. Because every Tier 1 submission uses at least the same per-cell sample size as a human half (see *Submission specification*), this is the natural point of comparison. It shows how well a fresh sample of this size reproduces the reference half, which is the target a strong silicon sample aims to match. We renamed it from v1's "ceiling," which wrongly suggested the best any approach could reach. It simply measures how well two independent human halves of this size agree. The reference is computed with the identical code path and reported in the same row format as every team. It is the one fixed reference row in every results table. A **floor baseline** ("no effect") anchors the other end for the metrics without a natural null (see *Cross-team comparison*). Because the split seed is itself an analytic choice, its influence is checked and reported: the reference (and, with real data, the full field) is recomputed across many alternative half-splits, so that results do not hinge on the seed we happened to draw (*Robustness of the scoring pipeline*, below). Every submission is scored under identical rules and appears on the leaderboard like any other. No entry receives a special reference row beyond the reference and floor.

**Scope: aggregate and distributional fidelity, not individual fidelity.** The benchmark tests whether an approach reproduces the megastudy's *estimands* (treatment effects, subgroup patterns, and population response distributions), not whether any synthetic respondent matches any real person. It cannot test the latter by design: teams build their own synthetic respondents, with no pairing between a synthetic and a human individual, so an individual-level claim is never made or scored. The distinction matters beyond bookkeeping. Recent work shows that aggregate agreement can coexist with divergent individual-level behaviour: simulated populations that reproduce collective outcomes while getting individual heterogeneity and decision rules wrong [a "macro–micro dissociation"; @arrudaCollectiveCooperationIndividual2026]. Benchmarks that do score individual fidelity require paired designs with per-person consistency ceilings [@parkLLMAgentsGrounded2026]. Our distribution metrics (variance ratio, OVL, KS) are the *population-level* response to that warning. They catch a synthetic crowd whose members answer too much alike, but they should not be read as evidence about individuals.

Which of the analyses below applies to a given submission follows from its tier (see *Analysis eligibility by tier*): Tier 1 is eligible for all of them, Tiers 2–3 for the estimate-based subset.

### Estimation functions

Every effect size compared below is produced by the same model functions, applied in the same way to the human reference and to each submission: average treatment effects per intervention for Section 1, and condition × moderator interactions for Section 2. These functions are unchanged from v1, and their full statistical rationale is in the [human study preregistration](https://doi.org/10.5281/zenodo.20160212). We reproduce them here so this document stands alone.

`run_main_treatment_model()` estimates each intervention's ATE against the shared control by OLS with HC2 heteroskedasticity-robust standard errors and BH-adjusted p-values.

```{r, results='asis'}
#| label: fn-run-main-treatment-model
print_a_function_from_file("run_main_treatment_model")
```

`run_main_treatment_model_binary()` is the counterpart for the binary newsletter-signup outcome. It fits a logistic regression and returns marginal effects on the probability scale.

```{r, results='asis'}
#| label: fn-run-main-treatment-model-binary
print_a_function_from_file("run_main_treatment_model_binary")
```

`run_moderator_model()` estimates the condition × moderator interactions used for the subgroup analysis (Section 2). Passing `compute_predicted = FALSE` skips the marginal-effects step the leaderboard does not use. For the binary newsletter-signup outcome the benchmark's subgroup scoring runs this same OLS function as a **linear probability model**: in the saturated condition × moderator specification the interaction coefficient equals the difference-in-differences of cell signup proportions (the interaction on the probability scale), with HC2 robust standard errors valid for a binary response — keeping every interaction estimate in a unit that pp-rescales like the sliders.

```{r, results='asis'}
#| label: fn-run-moderator-model
print_a_function_from_file("run_moderator_model")
```


### Comparison functions

The comparison metrics are implemented by a small set of helper functions, applied symmetrically to the human reference and to each submission. They are unchanged from v1 and shown here in full.

#### Comparison metrics

`compare_estimates()` is used throughout: for ATEs (Section 1), moderator interaction estimates (Section 2), and secondary analyses. It computes six metrics, reported in increasing order of strictness. The ordering locates *where* an approach breaks down. A submission might pass the weakest test (getting directions right) while failing a stricter one (magnitude equivalence), and knowing at which rung the ladder breaks is more informative than any single pass/fail verdict.

- **Directional agreement (%)**, *least strict*: % of estimates with the same sign as the human estimate. Chance is 50%. Do approaches at least identify the direction of each effect?
- **Spearman ρ**: rank correlation of point estimates. Do approaches rank interventions in the same order as humans? Insensitive to scale or offset. An approach that inflates all effects equally still scores ρ = 1.
- **Pearson r**: linear correlation of point estimates. Captures proportionality between predicted and human estimates but not absolute level.
- **Inferential agreement (%)**: % of estimates with the same BH-adjusted conclusion (significantly positive, significantly negative, or non-significant at α = .05). Requires getting both magnitude and uncertainty right. Would a researcher using the submission reach the same inferential decision as one using human data?
- **RMSE**: root mean squared error. Average absolute magnitude error. In the cross-team analysis every estimate is expressed in **percentage points (pp) of its outcome's scale range** before scoring (see *Scoring principle*, below), so RMSE reads in pp and is reported both pooled across outcomes and per outcome — matching @ashokkumarLargeLanguageModels2026a, whose primary archive reports RMSE pooled across experiments in exactly these units. As a scale reference, RMSE is read against the **spread (SD) of the human ATEs**: the spread is the error of the best possible constant prediction, so a submission with intervention-specific skill must come in below it. (In v1, which scores each outcome on its own raw scale, RMSE stays per-outcome and is omitted from the pooled row.)
- **TOST (%)**, *most strict*: % of estimate pairs passing an equivalence test (two one-sided tests, α = .05) with a **fixed, prespecified bound of 3% of each outcome's scale range** — a uniform Δ = 3 once effects are in pp (3 points on the 0–100 sliders, $0.30 on the donation, 3 percentage points on the signup proportion). This replaces v1's relative bound Δ_k = 0.5 × |ATE_human_k| for two preregistered reasons. First, a bound computed from the noisy estimate under test is not a valid α-level equivalence procedure (equivalence bounds must be set independently of the observed data). Second, the design analysis (see *Cross-team comparison*) shows the relative bound is smaller than the smallest difference the test could confirm at this design's reference SEs, so that rung would read ~0% for every entry. The v1 analysis (locked) retains its original bound. The fixed bound governs all cross-team scoring.

```{r, results='asis'}
#| label: fn-compare-estimates
print_a_function_from_file("compare_estimates")
```

#### Adjusted correlation and RMSE (noise-corrected)

The reference effects any submission is scored against are themselves noisy: each human ATE is estimated from a finite sample (and, here, from a *half*-split of it), so it carries a known standard error. That noise mechanically dilutes the correlation rungs. It cannot add to the covariance between predictions and effects (it is random, so unrelated to the predictions), but it inflates the observed spread of the reference effects, so the raw Pearson r is biased toward zero. The same noise inflates the RMSE.

We therefore report, as a **companion to the raw values wherever Pearson r appears in the cross-team analysis**, a measurement-error-adjusted correlation **r~adj~** and an adjusted RMSE (`adjusted_metrics()`). The estimand is deliberately framed in **fixed-effects** terms: *the correlation between the predictions and these same 16 true effects, free of the reference sample's measurement noise*. No assumption that the interventions' effects are draws from a common distribution is needed. The computation is the variance-decomposition identity (Spearman disattenuation with known error variances): the reference effects' true spread is their observed spread minus the average known sampling variance, and the adjusted correlation divides the (noise-immune) covariance by that corrected spread. This is the fixed-effects, method-of-moments analogue of the two-stage meta-analytic r~adj~ of @ashokkumarLargeLanguageModels2026a. With the benchmark's near-equal per-arm sample sizes, the precision weighting their likelihood machinery adds is immaterial, and our approach avoids its bivariate-normal assumption. Adjusted RMSE applies the same measurement-error logic to the RMSE rung: the average squared prediction error minus the average reference sampling variance, square-rooted.

Three disciplining rules govern its use. **Raw r remains the primary, ranked metric**: it is assumption-free, and because reference noise is roughly uniform across interventions it deflates every submission *and the human replication reference* together without reordering them. **r~adj~ is always shown with its uncertainty interval** (the same cluster bootstrap as every other metric): when true effect heterogeneity is small relative to the noise, the adjustment is intrinsically fragile, and the interval announces that rather than hiding it. **The adjustment is undefined when noise swamps signal**: if the estimated true variance is not positive, `adjusted_metrics()` returns NA instead of a fabricated value (on pure-noise placeholder data this guard binds by construction). Only the reference (Human 1) side is corrected. This treats every submission and the replication row the same way. Each has its Human 1 noise removed, and each keeps the noise on its own side, whether that is a submission's synthetic sampling noise or Human 2's sampling noise. A team lowers its own-side noise by using more synthetic respondents, which we encourage (see *Submission specification*), following @ashokkumarLargeLanguageModels2026a, who kept their predictions precise by averaging many simulated responses rather than correcting noisy ones. The adjustment matters most in the **subgroup analyses**, where small demographic cells make reference noise non-uniform and raw correlations unevenly deflated across exactly the stereotyping-relevant comparisons. There r~adj~ is the emphasized value, with raw r beside it. (Spearman ρ is attenuated by reference noise in the same direction but has no analogous closed-form correction, so it is reported raw only. The asymmetry is one more reason the ladder is read as a whole rather than any single rung.)

A companion benefit is comparability: @ashokkumarLargeLanguageModels2026a report r and r~adj~ side by side throughout. Across their megastudies, the closest analogue to this benchmark^[We take Ashokkumar et al.'s megastudy figures, not their headline r = .85, as the reference because the two numbers measure different tasks. The .85 is a correlation over 469 effects pooled from 70 unrelated survey experiments, spanning many topics, treatments, and outcomes. Their megastudy figures instead measure how well the model distinguishes the several interventions of a single megastudy against that study's shared control, which is exactly this benchmark's setting (16 interventions, one control). Ashokkumar et al. report the megastudies as a separate secondary archive (15 megastudies, 606 effects) and note that these correlations run lower than the primary archive's.], survey experiments reach r = .43 (r~adj~ = .52) and text-based treatments r = .45 (r~adj~ = .54), well below the r = .85 (r~adj~ = .92) of their primary 70-experiment archive. Reporting both metrics places every submission, and the field as a whole, directly against these published reference points.

```{r, results='asis'}
#| label: fn-adjusted-metrics
print_a_function_from_file("adjusted_metrics")
```

#### Distribution comparison metrics

`compare_distributions()` compares response distributions for a single condition and outcome using three metrics, again least-to-most strict:

- **Variance ratio** (submission / human variance), *least strict*. Captures only relative spread: a ratio of 1 means equal dispersion and does not assess shape. A ratio below 1 is the widely reported failure mode of synthetic samples: under-dispersed responses.
- **OVL (overlapping coefficient)**: proportion of area shared between the two kernel density estimates (1 = identical distributions, 0 = no overlap). Summarises overall shape similarity.
- **KS D-statistic**, *most strict*. Maximum absolute gap between the two empirical CDFs (0 = indistinguishable CDFs everywhere). Sensitive to local departures OVL might average away.

```{r, results='asis'}
#| label: fn-compare-distributions
print_a_function_from_file("compute_ovl")
```

#### Calibration regression

Correlation tells us only whether the human and predicted effect-size vectors *covary*. It does not capture baseline differences or multiplicative scaling. An approach could inflate every effect by a factor of two and still score Pearson r = 1. Calibration regression separates two failure modes: additive bias and proportionality.

`run_calibration()` regresses human ATEs on predicted ATEs: `ATE_human = α + β × ATE_predicted`. The intercept α captures additive bias (α > 0 means the approach systematically underestimates, α < 0 that it overestimates). The slope β captures proportionality (β = 1 is perfect scaling, β > 1 means predicted effects are compressed, β < 1 exaggerated). We report 95% CIs for both and R² as the share of human-ATE variance linearly recoverable from the predictions. We then test calibration at two strictness levels. The **joint F-test of (α = 0, β = 1)** is the omnibus test of perfect calibration: whether the predictions can be read directly as human ATEs with no correction. The weaker **slope-only F-test of (β = 1)** tests whether the predictions are *proportionally* calibrated, permitting a constant additive offset. Proportional calibration is the more practically relevant criterion: additive bias is easy to correct with a single human anchor, whereas a slope distortion reflects a deeper failure. In the benchmark the regression runs with **HC2 heteroskedasticity-robust inference** (`robust = TRUE`), because the outcome side carries known, condition-varying sampling error that classical F-tests ignore. A precision-weighted variant (weights 1/SE²~h~, `weight_by_precision = TRUE`) is reported as a sensitivity check with the real data. Three caveats travel with every calibration verdict. The regression has only 16 points, so the tests have little power and **non-rejection is not evidence of calibration**. Reference-side noise in the regressor direction is absent here (the human ATEs are the outcome), but for Tiers 2–3 the *predicted* ATEs carry the team's epistemic uncertainty as regressor noise, attenuating β̂ toward zero and biasing the verdict toward "exaggerated". Finally, the leaderboard's β and R² columns are computed on the primary outcome only (stated in the table caption).

```{r, results='asis'}
#| label: fn-calibration
print_a_function_from_file("run_calibration")
```

#### Demographic baseline and predictability metrics

`compare_demographic_baselines()` computes, for each moderator, the Pearson correlation and RMSE between human and predicted cell means in the control condition. `compare_demographic_predictability()` fits **separate** OLS regressions of the outcome on each moderator plus condition fixed effects, returning R² and dummy-coded coefficients for humans and for the submission. `demographic_parity_gap()` condenses the per-group baseline errors into one fairness number per moderator: the gap between the worst- and best-served group (see *Section 3*).

```{r, results='asis'}
#| label: fn-demographic-baselines
print_a_function_from_file("compare_demographic_baselines")
```

```{r, results='asis'}
#| label: fn-demographic-predictability
print_a_function_from_file("compare_demographic_predictability")
```

```{r, results='asis'}
#| label: fn-demographic-parity-gap
print_a_function_from_file("demographic_parity_gap")
```

### Section 1 — Average Treatment Effects

**ATE recovery.** For each intervention, the average treatment effect (ATE) is the difference in mean outcome between participants assigned to that intervention and those in the control condition. ATEs are estimated with `run_main_treatment_model()` (OLS with HC2 heteroskedasticity-robust standard errors and BH-adjusted p-values), run on the human reference and on the submission separately for each preregistered outcome. For `newsletter_signup` (binary), `run_main_treatment_model_binary()` returns marginal effects on the probability scale. Before any cross-outcome scoring, every estimate and standard error is converted to **percentage points of its outcome's scale range** (see *Scoring principle*), so effects on the 0–100 sliders, the $0–10 donation, and the signup probability share one unit. The full six-metric ladder (`compare_estimates()`) and the calibration regression (`run_calibration()`) are then applied. Following v1, the ladder is reported pooled across all outcomes to maximize power, then broken down by outcome; RMSE is reported in pp, both pooled and per outcome.

**Top-intervention selection.** The correlation rungs summarize the whole effect pattern. The decision a many-arm megastudy exists to inform is narrower: *which interventions should one act on?* `topk_selection()` scores it directly: per outcome, an approach's top **k = 3** interventions by predicted ATE (≈ the top 20% of the 16 arms, in the spirit of the intervention-selection use case that motivates @ashokkumarLargeLanguageModels2026a) are compared with the truly best three by the human reference, via the **hit rate** (share of picks in the true best set, chance = 3/16) and the **regret ratio** (mean true ATE of the picked set / mean true ATE of the best set, where 1 = perfect pick, scale-free within outcome, undefined when the best achievable mean ATE is not positive). Available to **all tiers** (it needs only point predictions).

**Response distributions.** Even an approach that matches every ATE may distribute responses differently: two groups with the same mean can differ in spread, skewness, or tail shape. In the **control condition**, across all continuous outcomes, we compare the full response distribution (`compare_distributions()`: variance ratio, OVL, KS D). The **variance ratio is the headline diagnostic** of the three, with a stated directional expectation: the recurring failure mode of synthetic samples, reported independently across the recent evaluation literature [@ashokkumarLargeLanguageModels2026a; @cumminsThreatAnalyticFlexibility2025b; @parkLLMAgentsGrounded2026; @arrudaCollectiveCooperationIndividual2026], is **under-dispersion**, synthetic respondents answering too much alike (ratio < 1). `newsletter_signup` is excluded (kernel density is not meaningful for a binary outcome). This analysis requires respondent-level data and so is **Tier 1 only**. By-outcome breakdowns use the illustrative subset (multidimensional trust, donation to AMS, funding perceptions, general climate policy).

### Section 2 — Subgroup Effects

**Heterogeneity in treatment effects.** Interventions may work differently across demographic groups: a consensus message might shift trust more among Republicans than Democrats, or more among younger than older respondents. For each of six categorical moderators (gender, age band, race, education, income, partisan identity), condition × moderator interactions are estimated with `run_moderator_model()` on the human reference and on the submission, across the illustrative outcomes plus the binary newsletter signup (`compute_predicted = FALSE` skips the marginal-effects step the leaderboard does not use). For the binary outcome the same OLS fit is a linear probability model: in the saturated condition × moderator specification, the interaction coefficient equals the difference-in-differences of cell signup proportions — the interaction on the probability scale — and the HC2 robust standard errors remain valid for a binary response. As in Section 1, all interaction estimates are converted to percentage points of each outcome's scale range before pooling. The full set of interaction estimates is compared with the same ladder ordering as Section 1. Because these pairs carry no inferential category per interaction cell, the pooled subgroup score uses the reduced signed-effect ladder of directional agreement, Spearman ρ, and Pearson r (`signed_metrics()`), plus the adjusted correlation r~adj~ computed from the human interaction estimates' standard errors. The subgroup section is where the adjustment matters most and is the emphasized value there: small demographic cells make the reference noise non-uniform, so raw correlations are unevenly deflated across exactly the comparisons the stereotyping question cares about (see *Adjusted correlation and RMSE*). Eligible for Tiers 1–2.

**Response distributions by subgroup.** Mirroring Section 1, within each demographic subgroup in the control condition we compare response distributions (OVL, KS D, variance ratio), to catch an approach that reproduces the overall distribution while miscalibrating within-group spreads. Run for all outcomes, demonstrated on the primary outcome (`trust_multidimensional`). A minimum group size of 10 is required for stable kernel density estimation. **Tier 1 only.**

### Section 3 — Demographic Baseline Calibration

Section 2 tests whether an approach exaggerates *effect* heterogeneity. Section 3 asks a separate question: does it start from the right **baseline**? It uses only the control condition. An approach could assign far too little trust to Republicans, or too much to Democrats, before any treatment. That is a distinct failure mode: baseline stereotyping rather than effect stereotyping.

**Demographic baseline calibration.** For each moderator, we compute control-condition group means for humans and for the submission, then correlate and compare them (`compare_demographic_baselines()`: Pearson r and RMSE of cell means). High correlation and low RMSE mean the approach starts from the right response level for each demographic type. Eligible for Tiers 1–2.

**Demographic parity gap (DPD).** The baseline-calibration metrics average over groups, and an average can hide exactly what a fairness question asks about: an approach may serve most groups well while consistently missing one. Following the demographic-parity-difference summary of @parkLLMAgentsGrounded2026, `demographic_parity_gap()` reports, per moderator, the gap between the **worst- and best-served demographic group** (the difference between the largest and smallest absolute cell-mean error in the control condition), together with which groups those are, and the **worst-served group's absolute error** alongside it (a zero gap can also mean every group is served equally *badly*, which the worst-group error catches). Groups with fewer than 30 respondents on either side are skipped: a small-cell group mean has an SE of several scale points, and a max-over-groups error is a max-of-noise statistic that is mechanically positive even for a perfect approach. DPD values are read against the human-replication reference row, which carries the same problem. A large gap flags that baseline accuracy is concentrated in some groups, the pattern prior work finds for populations under-represented in training data. Eligible for Tiers 1–2.

**Demographic predictability.** For each of the six moderators we fit a **separate** OLS regression of the outcome on that moderator plus condition fixed effects, on the human reference and on the submission independently (`compare_demographic_predictability()`). Separate per-moderator regressions avoid conflating correlated predictors (race, income, education are themselves related) and give a clean answer: how much does, say, partisan identity alone explain outcome variance in humans versus in the submission? An R² substantially higher for the submission than for humans flags over-reliance on demographic stereotypes. The dummy-coded coefficients identify which groups are over- or under-weighted. Run for all outcomes, demonstrated on the primary outcome. **Tier 1 only.**

### Tier-specific estimand recovery

The comparison-metric ladder requires, per intervention × outcome, an estimate, a standard error, and an inferential category. How these are obtained depends on the tier:

- **Tier 1:** the submission runs through `run_main_treatment_model()` exactly like the human data. Estimates and HC2 robust standard errors fall out of the fitted models. Submission-side **inference is then standardized to the human design's precision**: each SE is rescaled by the two-cell design factor $\sqrt{(1/n_{h,k} + 1/n_{h,ctrl})\,/\,(1/n_{l,k} + 1/n_{l,ctrl})}$ and p-values recomputed (normal approximation, BH within outcome, mirroring the Tier-2 path). A team may simulate any pool size, so unstandardized significance would measure that choice rather than the method, and the Tier-1 precision requirement would mechanically hurt precise teams wherever a human effect is n.s. Point estimates are untouched, so the correlation, directional, and RMSE rungs are unaffected. The standardization makes the inferential and TOST columns comparable across tiers.
- **Tier 2:** the ATE for each intervention × outcome is the submitted cell mean minus the submitted control mean. Because only cell summaries are available, the preregistered OLS cannot be refit on a Tier 2 submission. Instead we compute the ATE's standard error from the submitted cell uncertainty intervals. Each cell interval implies a standard error on that cell mean, $\mathrm{SE}_{cell} = (\mathrm{pi}_{upper} - \mathrm{pi}_{lower}) / (2 \times z_{0.975})$, and the treatment and control cells combine in the usual way for a between-subjects contrast, $\mathrm{SE} = \sqrt{\mathrm{SE}_t^2 + \mathrm{SE}_c^2}$. The test statistic $z = \mathrm{ATE} / \mathrm{SE}$ is referred to the standard normal distribution for a two-sided p-value, $p = 2\,\Phi(-|z|)$. These p-values are BH-adjusted within outcome, mirroring the Tier 1 models. Subgroup ATEs and demographic baselines are computed analogously from the moderator cell file.
- **Tier 3:** the submitted `ate` is the estimate. Inferential agreement uses the submitted 95% uncertainty interval: an effect counts as significantly positive (negative) if the interval lies entirely above (below) zero, the direct analog of a two-sided test at α = .05, so the significance criterion matches the one applied to Tiers 1–2. For the TOST equivalence test, the interval implies a standard error, $\mathrm{SE} = (\mathrm{pi}_{upper} - \mathrm{pi}_{lower}) / (2 \times z_{0.975})$.

**The submitted intervals are also scored directly (Tiers 2–3).** An uncertainty interval that only feeds the inferential rung would create a gaming channel: arbitrarily wide intervals turn every prediction "n.s.", which matches a mostly-null world for free. Two preregistered scores close it (`pi_metrics()`). **PI coverage** is the share of reference ATEs falling inside the submitted (Tier 3) or implied (Tier 2) 95% intervals. Honest intervals land near 95%. The **Winkler interval score** is the interval's width plus $2/\alpha$ times the distance by which the reference value misses it, averaged over pairs. The Winkler score is a *proper* score, minimized in expectation by reporting one's true predictive interval, so neither padding intervals wide nor squeezing them tight pays. It is scale-bound and therefore computed on the 0–100 outcomes only. Both appear as leaderboard columns for Tiers 2–3. They are undefined for Tier 1 (whose SEs are sampling, not epistemic, uncertainty) and for the reference rows.

**Human-side missing data** are handled by complete-case estimation within each model (the `lm`/`glm` default), exactly as in v1 and the main megastudy's preregistered analysis. No imputation is used anywhere in the scoring pipeline.

### Cross-team comparison (new)

This is the new analytical contribution of this preregistration, and it reports **two findings**. The **headline finding** asks how well the *field* of approaches predicts the experiment at all: the **distribution of predictive quality** across submissions on each metric, its centre (mean, median), its spread, and how close the field comes to the human replication reference. The **secondary finding** ranks individual approaches against one another. It is a structural read of *which* approaches lead on *which* rung, deliberately not a hunt for a single champion.

Both findings score every submission with the same metric ladder specified under *Comparison functions* above, in increasing order of strictness: directional agreement, Spearman ρ, Pearson r (with the noise-corrected r~adj~ as its companion), inferential agreement, RMSE, and TOST equivalence. This lets approaches be compared directly and against the human replication reference. Knowing on which rung an approach breaks is more informative than any single pass/fail verdict. On top of the ladder, the calibration regression (human ATEs on predicted ATEs) separates additive bias (α) from proportionality (β). Distributional metrics (variance ratio, overlap coefficient, KS distance) test whether Tier 1 submissions reproduce response *shapes*, not just means. The stereotyping diagnostics ask whether demographic variables predict synthetic responses with the same magnitude as in humans, or whether simulated respondents over-rely on demographic cues.

**Scoring principle: one score per metric, pooled across outcomes, in one unit.** Each approach is scored on every rung of the ladder, with the intervention × outcome estimates **pooled across all 13 outcomes** into a single value per metric: the 11 continuous sliders plus the two behavioral outcomes (donation via the OLS model, newsletter signup via logistic marginal effects on the probability scale, as in v1's pooled row). Before pooling, every estimate and standard error is converted to **percentage points (pp) of its outcome's scale range** — the unit of the predicted-vs-observed analysis of @ashokkumarLargeLanguageModels2026a, whose primary archive pools effects only after this conversion and who avoid SD standardization because synthetic under-dispersion corrupts an SD denominator (the same rationale applies here). Pooling the raw mixed units instead would not be neutral: dollar and probability effects are numerically 10–100× smaller than slider effects, so the sliders would dominate every pooled correlation, and the behavioral outcomes would collapse into a tight cluster at the origin that any submission — including one predicting zero for them — gets "right" for free. The conversion is a modelling choice, stated plainly: it equates 3 points on a 0–100 slider with $0.30 on the donation and 3 pp of signup probability, the same equation the TOST bound already makes. We deliberately do *not* collapse the rungs into one composite "champion" score. An approach can recover the *direction* of effects well while mis-scaling them, or do well on the primary outcome while failing on the rest, and a single number would hide exactly the structure the benchmark exists to reveal. Results are therefore read **per metric**: each panel is its own story. Pooling across outcomes (rather than averaging per-outcome scores) keeps every intervention × outcome comparison as one data point and matches the pooled-row logic of the ATE analysis in Section 1. Pooling has one known artifact, and we report its diagnostic: a pooled correlation can be inflated by a submission that predicts *which outcomes* are generally movable while misranking the interventions within every outcome. The leaderboard therefore carries the **Fisher-z average of the per-outcome correlations** beside the pooled value (`fisher_z_avg()`). When the two diverge, that is the signature of exactly this artifact, and the per-outcome breakdown shows where. With all estimates in pp, RMSE and its noise-corrected companion RMSE~adj~ are comparable across outcomes and are reported **pooled as well as per outcome** (in v1's raw-unit, per-outcome framing they could not be pooled). The per-intervention breakdown keeps its restriction to `outcomes_continuous`: the pp conversion removes the *unit* mix, but a per-intervention correlation across all 13 outcomes would still mix attitude and behavioral constructs, so the conservative subset is retained.

**Unit of analysis and coverage rules.** Teams may enter up to two submissions per tier, so submissions are not independent draws of "an approach": sibling entries from one team would overweight prolific teams in the field distribution and inflate its maximum. The **field statistics (Finding 1) therefore use one pre-designated primary entry per team**. Every valid entry still appears on the leaderboard (Finding 2), labelled primary or secondary. Declared coverage subsets are permitted, but a self-chosen easy subset could top a pooled correlation without being comparable to full-coverage entries. The **primary leaderboard is therefore restricted to full-coverage entries**. Partial-coverage entries are reported in a separate annex, scored pairwise on what they cover, with their coverage stated. Finally, the field distribution describes an invited, self-selected set of expert teams: a purposive sample of the current generation of serious approaches, not a random sample of all possible ones. We state this wherever the field summary is interpreted, and we do not display a field summary at all if fewer than **five** primary entries are scored.

**Presentation.** Every preregistered metric is computed and reported, but a figure that shows six metrics at once shows none of them well, so the figures carry only **one key metric per question**, stated here once and used consistently throughout. **Pearson *r*** is the key metric for ATE recovery (Section 1) and, on the same axis, for subgroup recovery (Section 2). The **calibration slope β and intercept α** answer whether those effects are the right *size*, not only correctly ranked. The **variance ratio** is the key metric for response-shape recovery (Section 1b/2b). Every other rung of the preregistered ladder (directional agreement, Spearman ρ, the adjusted correlations, inferential and TOST agreement, RMSE, OVL, KS, W1) is still computed and scored for every submission, and reported in a companion table beside each figure. Nothing is dropped, only de-emphasized in the plots.

The headline display (@fig-headline) puts the key measures in one figure with two panels. Panel A plots predicted against human ATEs across all approaches and outcomes, in percentage points of each outcome's scale range. This is the direct visual of the prediction task, in the units of the predicted-vs-observed figure of @ashokkumarLargeLanguageModels2026a (effects on the original scales, no SD standardization, which an under-dispersed submission would corrupt). It is overlaid with the average field and human **calibration regressions** and a corner readout of their correlation and calibration coefficients (slope β, perfect = 1, and intercept α, perfect = 0, with 95% CIs). Panel B **ranks** the approaches on the pooled Pearson *r*, each with a cluster-bootstrap **uncertainty interval** over interventions (introduced by this preregistration, since v1 reported point estimates only, and detailed under *Uncertainty intervals* below), with the human replication reference pinned on top and an **all-approaches summary row** at the bottom carrying the field's density and mean with its CI. This one panel carries both the field distribution (Finding 1) and the ranking (Finding 2). Three further figures break the ATE and shape key metrics down: by outcome and by intervention (@fig-field-by-group), by moderator for subgroup recovery (@fig-subgroup-headline), and per-outcome response densities with the variance ratio and Wasserstein-1 distance stamped on each panel (@fig-distribution-headline). Every entry carries its disclosure-class badge (open / escrowed / sealed) in the leaderboard table, and the precise numbers accompany every figure as a companion table.

#### Uncertainty intervals on the leaderboard

**Why the leaderboard carries intervals at all.** Each point in the ranking panel is a single pooled score, one number per approach per metric. A bare point invites over-reading: if one approach scores Pearson *r* = 0.60 and another 0.55, is the first genuinely better, or is the gap an artifact of the particular interventions that happened to be tested? The interval answers exactly that question. It is the guard against ranking approaches on differences that would not survive a different, equally reasonable test.

**What the interval is, mechanically.** The pooled metric is computed once on the full data to give the point estimate. We then form a *cluster bootstrap over interventions*: the 16 interventions are resampled with replacement (some drawn twice, some dropped), the pooled metric is recomputed on each resampled set, and the 2.5th–97.5th percentiles of those recomputed scores give the 95% interval (`cluster_boot()` in `R/functions/statistics.R`). Clustering is *by intervention* (each intervention's full block of outcome rows is drawn or dropped together) because the outcome rows belonging to one intervention are not independent. **No submitted prediction is ever altered**: each approach's predicted effect for each intervention stays fixed at the value the team submitted. What is resampled is *which interventions enter the score*, not how well anyone did on them. The interval therefore measures the spread of the recomputed *score*, not the spread of the raw effects: averaging or correlating cancels much of the effect-to-effect spread, which is why the bar is typically far narrower than the range of the underlying estimates.

**What the interval means, and the one assumption it rests on.** The interval treats the 16 interventions as a *test set* standing in for the broader space of interventions the benchmark cares about, exactly as a held-out test set stands in for future cases in predictive modelling. Read this way, the bar answers a generalisation question: *would this approach's score, and its rank, survive on a different set of interventions of the same kind?* This is the appropriate question for a benchmark, whose purpose is to identify general predictive skill rather than performance on sixteen specific stimuli. The assumption is therefore about the *interventions*, not the predictions: that the tested set is a sample from a population of possible interventions. Resampling does **not** assume that a team that predicted a given intervention well "could have" gotten it wrong (the predictions are fixed). It assumes only that a replication might have tested a different set of interventions. (If one instead regarded these exact sixteen as the entire universe of interest, there would be no sampling and hence no uncertainty to display. The scores would simply be exact. We do not adopt that reading: the benchmark exists to compare *approaches*, not to characterise these particular stimuli.)

**Interval width is informative, not a penalty on accuracy.** Because the metrics are bounded agreement scores with a ceiling at perfect agreement, an approach that tracks the human effects closely sits on the diagonal and stays there under resampling: dropping or duplicating interventions barely moves a score already pinned near perfect agreement. Accurate approaches therefore carry *narrow* intervals. An approach whose predictions do not track the human effects has a score floating in the interior, free to swing with the resampled set, and so carries a *wide* interval. Interval width is thus entangled with accuracy, but in the helpful direction: a wide bar is the leaderboard signalling that a score is flimsy and may reflect test-set luck rather than skill, precisely the warning one wants attached to a weak entry. (Spread in the size of the true effects does not by itself widen the bar. The correlation and agreement metrics are scale-free, so what matters is whether predictions hug the line, not how far apart the interventions are. Width also partly reflects differences in per-intervention difficulty, so it is a flimsiness *signal*, not a pure standard error.)

**Decision rule for ranking claims: paired differences on shared replicates.** Overlapping marginal bootstrap intervals do **not** mean "A and B are indistinguishable", and non-overlap is not required to claim a difference. That is the standard misreading. Because every submission is scored against the *same* reference on the *same* intervention clusters with the *same* preregistered bootstrap seed, the correct comparison is paired: draw one set of clusters per replicate, score both submissions on it, and take the difference, so the shared reference noise cancels within each replicate (`cluster_boot_diff()`). **We preregister the rule that any claim that one approach outperforms another on a metric requires the paired-difference 95% interval to exclude zero**. Otherwise the two are reported as statistically indistinguishable on that metric, the most common outcome among top performers in comparable mass-collaboration benchmarks (Salganik et al. 2020), and one we expect here. Two caveats apply to all bootstrap intervals: with 16 intervention clusters, percentile intervals are known to undercover somewhat, and the 16 ATEs share the single control arm, which induces positive dependence the intervention-level resampling does not capture. The intervals are read as honest approximations, not exact coverage statements.

**Relation to the per-effect confidence and uncertainty intervals.** The bootstrap interval is a *different layer* of uncertainty from the confidence intervals (Tier 1) and uncertainty intervals (Tiers 2–3) attached to individual treatment effects, and the two should not be conflated. A per-effect CI or PI is *within-intervention*: it says how precisely a single effect is known, whether from finite-respondent sampling noise for a Tier 1 model fit or from the team's own stated uncertainty for a Tier 2/3 submission. The bootstrap interval is *across-intervention*: it says how much the pooled score depends on which interventions were tested. The two are **nested, not competing**. The per-effect intervals enter the *value* of each row. For the correlation and directional rungs only the point estimates are used, so the per-effect intervals do not affect those scores at all and the bootstrap is their sole source of an error bar. For the inferential-agreement and TOST rungs the per-effect intervals set each row's significance or equivalence label, which the bootstrap then pools and resamples. In all cases the bootstrap holds each intervention's estimate fixed at its point value: it propagates the *which-interventions* uncertainty into the bars but **not** the within-effect sampling noise.

**A noted alternative.** Folding both layers into a single bar would require a *two-level* bootstrap: in each replicate, resample the interventions *and* perturb each drawn effect by its standard error (drawing the effect from a normal centred on the estimate with that SE).
